Ahmose I.detailed_knowledge:
Ahmose I (Amosis, Aahmes; meaning "Iah (the Moon) is born") was a pharaoh and founder of the Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt in the New Kingdom of Egypt, the era in which ancient Egypt achieved the peak of its power. His reign is usually dated to the mid-16th century BC at the beginning of the Late Bronze Age. During his rule, Ahmose completed the conquest and expulsion of the Hyksos, restored Theban rule over Lower and Upper Egypt, and successfully reasserted Egyptian power in its formerly subject territories of Nubia and Canaan. He then reorganized the administration of the country, reopened quarries, mines and trade routes, and began massive construction projects of a type that had not been undertaken since the time of the Middle Kingdom. This building program culminated in the construction of the last pyramid built by native Egyptian rulers. Ahmose's reign laid the foundations for the New Kingdom, under which Egyptian power reached its peak.

Ahmose was a member of the Theban royal house, the son of pharaoh Seqenenre Tao and brother of the last pharaoh of the Seventeenth Dynasty, Kamose. When he was seven years old, his father was killed, and he was about ten when his brother died after reigning only three years. Upon coronation, Ahmose became known as Nebpehtyre, meaning "The Lord of Strength is Ra." Ahmose I followed the tradition of his father and married several of his sisters, making Ahmose-Nefertari his chief wife. Their son Ahmose-ankh was heir apparent but preceded his father in death, and Ahmose was ultimately succeeded by his eldest surviving son, Amenhotep I.

Ahmose I ascended the throne as a boy king at the age of ten and ruled for approximately twenty-five years before dying around the age of thirty-five. The beginning of his reign was conducted under the regency of his mother Ahhotep, who effectively consolidated the Theban power base until Ahmose assumed full control. The conflict between the local kings of Thebes and the Hyksos king Apepi had begun during the reign of Ahmose's father and was concluded, after almost thirty years of intermittent conflict, during his own reign.

Ahmose began the conquest of Lower Egypt, held by the Hyksos, starting around the eleventh year of the Hyksos king Khamudi's reign. Records of the campaign were found on the tomb walls of a participating soldier, Ahmose son of Ebana, indicating that Ahmose I led three attacks against Avaris, the Hyksos capital, before conquering it on the fourth. Entering Heliopolis first, he moved down the eastern delta to take Tjaru, the major border fortification on the Horus Road — the road from Egypt to Canaan — thereby cutting off all traffic between Canaan and Avaris and isolating the Hyksos capital from reinforcement or supplies. He completed his victory by conquering the Hyksos stronghold of Sharuhen near Gaza after a three-year siege.

After defeating the Hyksos, Ahmose campaigned in both Syria and Nubia. A campaign during his twenty-second year reached Djahy in the Levant and possibly as far as the Euphrates. In Nubia, Ahmose crushed two successive rebellions and restored Egyptian rule, administered from a new centre established at Buhen.

With the reunification of Upper and Lower Egypt, a renewal of royal support for the arts and monumental construction followed. With the Delta and Nubia under Egyptian control, access was gained to resources not previously available: gold and silver from Nubia, lapis lazuli from central Asia, cedar from Byblos, and turquoise from the Sinai mines at Serabit el-Khadim. The art of glassmaking is thought to have developed during Ahmose's reign. He made additions to the temple of Amun at Karnak and to the temple of Montu at Armant, and built a cenotaph for his grandmother Queen Tetisheri at Abydos. Excavations at Avaris have shown that Ahmose had a palace constructed on the site of the former Hyksos capital, its walls once decorated with Minoan-style frescoes. Under his reign, Thebes became the capital and religious centre of all Egypt, its local god Amun credited with inspiring his victories over the Hyksos.

The remains of Ahmose's pyramid at Abydos were discovered in 1899. This pyramid was the last ever built as part of a mortuary complex in Egypt. Subsequent pharaohs abandoned the pyramid form for both practical and religious reasons: the geography of Thebes was ill-suited to pyramid construction, burials in the surrounding desert were vulnerable to flooding, and the pyramid form was associated with the sun god Ra, who had been overshadowed by Amun. All subsequent New Kingdom pharaohs were buried in rock-cut shaft tombs in the Valley of the Kings.

Ahmose I's mummy was discovered in 1881 within the Deir el-Bahri Cache. It was unwrapped by Gaston Maspero on 9 June 1887, found within a coffin bearing his name in hieroglyphs. The body stood 1.63 metres in height and bore signs of ancient plundering. Maspero noted extraordinary strength in the development of the neck and chest, and remarked that the face closely resembled that of his father Seqenenre Tao. Later medical examination suggested Ahmose died in his mid-thirties. The mummy is now in the Luxor Museum as part of a permanent exhibition called The Golden Age of the Egyptian Military.

Akhenaten.detailed_knowledge: Akhenaten (also spelled Akhenaton, Echnaton), meaning "Effective for the Aten," was an ancient Egyptian pharaoh reigning c. 1353-1336 BC, the tenth ruler of the Eighteenth Dynasty. Originally named Amenhotep IV, in the fifth year of his reign he adopted the name Akhenaten. As a pharaoh, Akhenaten is noted for abandoning the traditional polytheistic religion of ancient Egypt and introducing Atenism worship centred around the solar disc, the Aten. Whether this religious policy was truly monotheistic or rather henotheistic remains a subject of scholarly debate. This profound cultural shift was reversed after his death. Akhenaten's monuments were dismantled and hidden, his statues destroyed, and his name excluded from lists of rulers compiled by later pharaohs. He was all but lost to history until the late 19th-century discovery of Amarna the new capital city he had built for the worship of the Aten. The future Akhenaten was born Amenhotep, a younger son of pharaoh Amenhotep III and his principal wife, Tiye. His elder brother, crown prince Thutmose, was originally Amenhotep III's heir, but Thutmose's early death placed Akhenaten next in line for the throne. Akhenaten married Nefertiti as his Great Royal Wife; together they had six daughters, including Meritaten, Meketaten, and Ankhesenamun, who would later become the wife of Tutankhamun. A secondary wife named Kiya is also known from inscriptions, and some Egyptologists theorize she was the mother of Tutankhamun. Genetic testing has suggested that Tutankhamun's father was a male individual buried in tomb KV55 in the Valley of the Kings, most likely identified as Akhenaten. Akhenaten took Egypt's throne as Amenhotep IV, most likely in 1353 or 1351 BC. The beginning of his reign followed established pharaonic traditions; he did not immediately redirect worship toward the Aten, and early inscriptions show him worshipping several traditional gods including Atum, Osiris, Anubis, and Hathor. He continued his father's construction projects at Karnak while simultaneously building new temples dedicated to the Aten at cities across Egypt. These Aten temples had no roof, so that the god was worshipped in open sunlight a marked departure from the dark enclosed sanctuaries of traditional Egyptian worship. In his fifth regnal year, Amenhotep IV changed his name to Akhenaten and decreed the construction of a new capital city: Akhetaten, meaning "Horizon of the Aten," known today as Amarna. The pharaoh chose a site roughly halfway between Thebes and Memphis on the east bank of the Nile, previously uninhabited and unclaimed by any god or ruler. The city was built rapidly using a standardised smaller building block called the talatat. By his eighth regnal year, Akhetaten was sufficiently complete to be occupied by the royal family. By his ninth regnal year, Akhenaten declared the Aten the only worshippable deity. He ordered the defacing of Amun's temples throughout Egypt and banned images of all gods except the rayed solar disc whose rays were depicted as long arms terminating in human hands, symbolising the Aten's invisible, life-giving spirit. Akhenaten declared himself the sole intermediary between the Aten and humanity. His beliefs are best expressed in the Great Hymn to the Aten, which proclaims: "O Sole God beside whom there is none! You made the earth as you wished, you alone." The Amarna letters a cache of 382 diplomatic texts discovered between 1887 and 1979 provide the most important evidence for Akhenaten's foreign policy, recording correspondence between Egypt and the rulers of Babylonia, Assyria, Syria, Canaan, Mitanni, and the Hittites. Egypt was unable or unwilling to oppose the growing strength of the Hittite Empire, and several vassals switched their allegiances. Akhenaten repeatedly failed to respond to urgent pleas for military assistance, most notably from Rib-Hadda of Byblos, who wrote sixty letters before being captured and executed. The only confirmed military campaign of his reign was a successful expedition to Nubia to suppress a rebellion. Akhenaten died in his seventeenth regnal year and was initially buried in a tomb in the Royal Wadi east of Akhetaten. His mummy was almost certainly later moved to tomb KV55 in the Valley of the Kings, though its identification as Akhenaten remains a subject of scholarly debate. Following Akhenaten's death, the Aten cult fell rapidly out of favour. His successor Tutankhamun restored the traditional gods and reestablished their temples. Later pharaohs particularly Horemheb and Seti I systematically erased Akhenaten from the historical record, demolishing Aten temples and removing him from official king lists. Later rulers referred to him as "the enemy of Akhetaten" or simply "that criminal." Some Egyptologists argue that his reign initiated a gradual decline in the pharaoh's divine authority: by banning traditional festivals and declaring himself the sole conduit to the divine, he severed the bond between ordinary Egyptians and their gods, a shift that eventually contributed to the growing political power of the Amun priesthood. The art produced during Akhenaten's reign, known as Amarna art, departs markedly from traditional Egyptian conventions. Representations are more realistic, naturalistic, and expressive, conveying movement and intimacy particularly in depictions of the royal family in informal, affectionate settings. Akhenaten himself is portrayed with an elongated skull, sagging stomach, broad hips, and androgynous features a deliberate stylistic choice most scholars interpret as symbolising the androgynous, all-encompassing nature of the Aten rather than any physical condition. He remains among the most debated and fascinating figures in all of ancient history.

Al-Azhar Mosque.detailed_knowledge:
Al-Azhar Mosque, known in Egypt simply as al-Azhar, is a mosque in the historic Islamic core of the city of Cairo, Egypt. Commissioned as the new capital of the Fatimid Caliphate in 970 CE, it was the first mosque established in a city that eventually earned the nickname "the City of a Thousand Minarets." Its name is usually thought to derive from az-Zahra, a title given to Fatima, the daughter of Muhammad. The affiliated Al-Azhar University is the second oldest continuously operating university in the world. It has long been regarded as the foremost institution in the Islamic world for the study of Sunni theology and sharia. Today, al-Azhar remains a deeply influential institution in Egyptian society, highly revered throughout the Sunni Muslim world and a symbol of Islamic Egypt.

The city of Cairo was established by the Fatimid general Jawhar al-Siqilli following the Fatimid conquest of Egypt in 969. Jawhar ordered the construction of a congregational mosque and work commenced on 4 April 970. Built from limestone of the Mokattam Formation, the mosque was completed in 972 and the first Friday prayers were held on 22 June 972 during Ramadan. Al-Azhar soon became a centre of learning in the Islamic world. In 988, Yaqub ibn Killis made al-Azhar a key centre for instruction in Islamic law, and the following year forty-five scholars were hired to give lessons — laying the foundation for what would become the leading university in the Muslim world.

The mosque was expanded under the caliph al-Aziz Billah and renovated by al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah. Caliph al-Hafiz undertook a major refurbishment in 1138, establishing the keel-shaped arches and carved stucco decoration seen in the courtyard today. Al-Azhar was endowed with thousands of manuscripts in 1005, forming the basis of its library.

Saladin, who overthrew the Fatimids in 1171, was hostile to the Shiite traditions of al-Azhar, and under his Ayyubid dynasty the mosque suffered neglect — congregational prayers were banned and student funding withdrawn. Congregational prayers were reestablished by Sultan Baibars in 1266 under the Mamluk Sultanate, which also restored stipends for students and teachers and undertook extensive repairs. The sultans Qaytbay and Qansuh al-Ghuri each erected minarets that still stand today. By the 14th century, al-Azhar had become the foremost centre for studies in law, theology, and Arabic in the Islamic world.

With the Ottoman annexation of Egypt in 1517, direct royal patronage ceased, though Ottoman governors still attended Friday prayers at al-Azhar. The 18th century saw a major expansion under Abd al-Rahman Katkhuda, who nearly doubled the mosque's size, adding three new gates and a second prayer hall.

Napoleon invaded Egypt in July 1798 and established a governing council of nine al-Azhar sheikhs. A revolt against French troops was launched from al-Azhar on 21 October 1798; when negotiations failed, Napoleon ordered the city bombarded, targeting al-Azhar directly. French troops desecrated the mosque, tying their horses to the mihrab and ransacking the libraries. The mosque remained closed until August 1801.

Following the Egyptian Revolution of 1952, the university was officially separated from the mosque and designated an independent institution in 1961, with new secular faculties added in medicine, engineering, and economics. Student numbers grew from under 90,000 in 1970 to over 1.3 million by 2001. Under Presidents Nasser, Sadat, and Mubarak, al-Azhar was progressively integrated into the state apparatus while retaining its status as the supreme religious authority of the Sunni Muslim world.

The original mosque measured 85 by 69 metres, comprising three arcades surrounding a courtyard and a hypostyle prayer hall. Its marble columns were reused from Pharaonic, Roman, and Coptic sites. The stucco exterior shows influences from Abbasid, Coptic, and Byzantine architecture. Notable surviving architectural elements include the Minaret of Qaytbay (1483-1495) with its three muqarnas-supported balconies, the double-finial Minaret of al-Ghuri (1509) decorated with blue faience, and the Bab al-Muzayinin (Gate of the Barbers, 1753), the present main entrance. In 2018, a major restoration financed by Saudi Arabia reinforced the building's foundations and restored its architectural elements. Al-Azhar continues to exert considerable influence on the Islamic world as the foremost Sunni religious authority.

Al-Muizz Street.detailed_knowledge: Al-Muizz li-Din Allah al-Fatimi Street, or al-Muizz Street for short, is a major north-to-south street in the walled city of historic Cairo, Egypt. It is one of Cairo's oldest streets and dates back to the foundation of the city by the Fatimid dynasty in the 10th century, under their fourth caliph, Al-Mu'izz li-Din Allah, after whom the street is named. Historically, it was the most important artery of the city and was often referred to as the Qasaba. It constituted the main axis of the city's economic zones where its souks were concentrated. The street's prestige also attracted the construction of many monumental religious and charitable buildings, making it a dense repository of historic Islamic architecture in Cairo, especially evident in the Bayn al-Qasrayn area. Al-Muizz street runs from the city gate of Bab al-Futuh in the north to the gate of Bab Zuweila in the south, approximately one kilometer long. The street was laid out at the very beginning of Cairo's foundation by the Fatimid dynasty, who conquered Egypt in 969 CE. In 970, the general Jawhar al-Siqilli planned and constructed a new city named al-Mu'izziyya al-Qaahirah, later simply called al-Qahira, giving us the modern name of Cairo. Jawhar organized the city so that two great palaces for the caliphs were at its center, with an important plaza known as Bayn al-Qasrayn between them. The city's main street connected its northern and southern gates and passed between the palaces. In this early period, Cairo was a restricted city accessible only to the caliph, the army, and state officials. After the demise of the Fatimid regime in 1171 under Saladin, the city was opened up to common people and underwent major transformations. The Ayyubid sultans and their Mamluk successors progressively demolished and replaced the Fatimid structures with their own buildings. The Qasaba avenue went from a partly ceremonial axis to a major commercial street with shops and souqs establishing themselves along most of its length. The Khan al-Khalili commercial district developed on the Qasaba's eastern side. During the Mamluk period in particular, the street filled up with major architectural monuments, many of which still stand today. In the 20th century, the construction of al-Azhar street created a major interruption in the traditional path of al-Muizz street, splitting the old city into two. Notable historic monuments along the street from north to south include: the Mosque of Al Hakim bi Amr Allah (1013), Bayt al-Suhaymi (1648-1796), Mosque of al-Aqmar (1125), Qasr Bashtak (1339), Madrasa of Barquq (1386), Complex of Qalawun (1285), Madrasa of Al-Salih Ayyub (1250), Khan al-Khalili, Madrasa of Sultan Al-Ghuri (1505), Mosque of Muayyad (1420), and the Mosque of Salih Tala'i (1160). Starting in 1997, the Egyptian government carried out extensive renovations to turn the street into an open-air museum, restoring 34 monuments along the street and 67 nearby, repaving in the original style, and installing a drainage system to protect against subterranean water, the principal threat to Islamic Cairo.

Alexandria Library.detailed_knowledge: The Bibliotheca Alexandrina is a major library and cultural center on the shore of the Mediterranean Sea in Alexandria, Egypt. It is a commemoration of the Library of Alexandria, once one of the largest libraries worldwide, which was lost in antiquity. The complex was officially inaugurated in 2002. At the time of its construction, the reading room was the largest in the world. The library offers shelf space for eight million books, containing books in the Egyptian, classical Arabic, English, and French languages. In 2009, the library received a donation of 500,000 books from the Bibliotheque nationale de France making the Bibliotheca Alexandrina the sixth-largest Francophone library in the world. The complex also houses a conference center, several specialized libraries, multiple museums and art galleries, a planetarium, and a manuscript restoration laboratory. The idea of reviving the Library of Alexandria dates back to 1974, when a committee set up by Alexandria University selected a plot of land for its new library between the campus and the seafront, close to where the ancient library once stood. One leading supporter of the project was former Egyptian President Hosni Mubarak, along with a partnership with UNESCO. UNESCO organized an architectural design competition in 1988 to choose the design. The competition was won by Snohetta, a Norwegian architectural office, among 524 other entries. UNESCO also created an International Commission for the Bibliotheca Alexandrina. The first pledges were made for funding the project at a conference held in 1990 in Aswan along the upper Nile River with US$65 million, mostly from the MENA states. Construction work began in 1995, and, after some additional US$220 million had been spent, the complex was officially inaugurated on 16 October 2002. The Internet Archive donated US$5 million to the BA, including 10 billion web pages from over 16 million sites, 2000 hours of Egyptian and U.S. television broadcasts, 1000 archival films, 100 terabytes of data stored on 200 computers, and a bookscanning facility for local books. The library also received a supplementary donation of 500,000 books from the Bibliotheque nationale de France in Paris. The library has shelf space for eight million books, with the main reading room covering 20,000 square metres on eleven cascading levels. At the time of construction, the reading room was the largest in the world. The complex also houses a conference center; specialized libraries for maps, multimedia, for serving the blind and visually impaired, young people, and children; four museums; four art galleries for temporary exhibitions; 15 permanent exhibitions; a planetarium; and a manuscript restoration laboratory. The main reading room stands beneath a 32-meter-high glass-panelled roof, tilted out toward the sea like a sundial, and measuring some 160 m in diameter. The walls are of gray granite, carved with characters from 120 scripts. The main library can hold up to eight million books. The library provides access to print on demand books via the Espresso Book Machine. The Taha Hussein Library contains materials for the blind and visually impaired using special software that makes it possible for readers to read books and journals. It is named after Taha Hussein, the Egyptian professor of Arabic and literary critic who was himself blinded at the age of three. Contains book collections of Nobel Prize Laureates in Literature from 1901. The Nobel Section was inaugurated by Queen Silvia of Sweden and Queen Sonja of Norway on 24 April 2002. The Antiquities Museum is an archeological museum established in 2001 situated within the library. It holds approximately 1,316 artifacts. The collection includes underwater antiquities from the Mediterranean seabed near the Eastern Harbour and the Bay of Abukir. The museum provides descriptions of artifacts in three languages: English, Arabic, and French. The Manuscript Museum provides visitors and researchers with rare manuscripts and books. Established in 2001, the Manuscript Museum contains the world's largest collection of digital manuscripts. The Sadat Museum contains many personal belongings of the Egyptian president, Anwar Sadat. The collections include some of his military robes, his Nobel Prize medal, his copy of the Quran, a few of his handwritten letters, pictures of him and his family, and the blood-stained military robe he wore on the day of his assassination. In 2023, the museum also retrieved the passport of the president from an online auction in the United States. The Our Digital World exhibition displays some of the library's digital projects, including digital archives of former presidents, the Science Supercourse, and the Encyclopedia of Life. The Culturama is a hall that consists of a 180-degree panoramic interactive computer screen with a diameter of 10 meters made up of nine separate flat screens arranged in a semicircle and nine video projectors controlled by a single computer. It was developed by the Egyptian Center for Documentation of Cultural and National Heritage and holds its patent in 2007. The Digital Assets Repository is a system developed at the Bibliotheca Alexandrina that maintains digital library collections as well as providing free public access to the library's digitized collections through web-based search and browsing facilities. Per Presidential Decree No. 76 in 2001, the Bibliotheca Alexandria is managed by a Council of Patrons, a Board of Trustees, and a Director. The founding director was Ismail Serageldin, who served until May 2017. He was succeeded by manager Moustafa El Feqy. While the library was shut down during the 2011 Egyptian revolution, young adults were seen in a circle around the building to protect against looters and vandals. Students from neighboring universities make up 80% of Bibliotheca Alexandrina patrons. Many allege that the library is a white elephant, which serves as little more than a vanity project for the Egyptian government. On its opening day, an exhibition in the library's Manuscript Center drew international criticism for including the anti-semitic fabricated text, The Protocols of the Elders of Zion, alongside the Bible and the Torah. In response to the criticism, the director of the library ordered that The Protocols be removed from the exhibition.

Amenemhat I.detailed_knowledge: Amenemhat I, also known as Amenemhet I, was a pharaoh of ancient Egypt and the first king of the Twelfth Dynasty of the Middle Kingdom. Amenemhat I was probably the same as the vizier named Amenemhat who led an expedition to Wadi Hammamat under his predecessor Mentuhotep IV, and possibly overthrew him from power. Scholars differ as to whether Mentuhotep IV was killed by Amenemhat I, but there is no independent evidence to suggest this and there may even have been a period of co-regency between their reigns. Amenemhat I was not of royal lineage, born to Senusret and Nefert who were possibly related to the nomarchial family of Elephantine. The composition of some literary works such as the Prophecy of Neferti and the Instructions of Amenemhat, and in architecture the reversion to the pyramid-style complexes of the 6th dynasty rulers, are often considered to have been attempts at legitimizing his rule. Many scholars in recent years have argued that Amenemhat I's mother was of Nubian origin. Amenemhat I moved the capital from Thebes to Itjtawy and was buried in el-Lisht. Both the Instructions of Amenemhat and the Story of Sinuhe suggest he was assassinated before he could celebrate his first Sed festival. Amenemhat I's praenomen occupies the first entry of the third row of the Karnak King List. In the Abydos king list, Amenemhat I's praenomen Sehetep-ib-re occupies the fifty-ninth entry. The Turin Canon partially preserves Amenemhat I's praenomen and accords him a reign of 29 years. Amenemhat is also mentioned in Manetho's Aegyptiaca, originally composed circa the 3rd century BC. Amenemhat was the son of a Senusret and a Nefret, who were not of the royal family. His mother is attested to on an offering table found at Amenemhat I's pyramid at El-Lisht. The relationship between Amenemhat I and his predecessor Mentuhotep IV remains unclear, although he may be identical to the vizier Amenemhat under Mentuhotep IV mentioned in inscriptions from Wadi Hammamat. The inscriptions record two prophetic events: the birth of a gazelle calf on the stone which became Mentuhotep IV's sarcophagus lid, and a sudden rainstorm that revealed a well brimming with water. There is also an inscription on a bowl from El-Lisht bearing the names of both kings. Amenemhat I had one presumed wife, Neferitatjenen, and one known son, his successor Senusret I. Three of his daughters are also known: Neferu III who was the wife of Senusret I, and Neferusherit and Kayet who are named on artifacts found in Amenemhat I's pyramid complex. There is some evidence that the early reign of Amenemhat I was beset with political turmoil, as indicated by the inscriptions of Nehri, a local governor. Amenemhat I's Horus name, Wehemmesu, means renaissance or rebirth, an allusion to the Old Kingdom period whose cultural icons and models were emulated by the Twelfth Dynasty kings. Amenemhat I built his pyramid at El-Lisht with a core constructed from roughly hewn limestone blocks with gaps plugged with sand, debris, and mudbrick. The pyramid had a base length of 84 m inclined at approximately 54 degrees toward an apex 55 to 58 m high and had a volume of 129,360 cubic meters. As a result of poor construction and the activity of grave robbers, the pyramid has now been reduced to a 20 m tall ruined mound. The Instructions of Amenemhat were supposedly counsels that the deceased king gave to his son during a dream, warning Senusret I against too great intimacy with his subjects and telling the story of his own death, referring to a conspiracy in which Amenemhat was killed by his own guards when his son was leading a campaign in Libya. Amenemhat I is considered to be the first king of Egypt to have had a coregency with his son Senusret I. A double dated stele from Abydos establishes that Senusret was made co-regent in Amenemhat's Year 20. Nobel Prize-winning Egyptian novelist Naguib Mahfouz includes Amenemhat I in his 1941 story Awdat Sinuhi, based on the Story of Sinuhe, and also includes the pharaoh in his account of Egypt's rulers Facing the Throne.

Amenhotep III.detailed_knowledge:
Amenhotep III, also known as Amenhotep the Magnificent, was the ninth pharaoh of the Eighteenth Dynasty. According to different chronologies, he ruled Ancient Egypt from approximately 1386 to 1349 BC, following the death of his father Thutmose IV. His reign marked a time of exceptional prosperity and grandeur, during which Egypt reached the height of its artistic and international influence. He is one of the few pharaohs worshipped as a deity during his own lifetime. When he died in the 38th or 39th year of his reign, he was succeeded by his son Amenhotep IV, who later changed his name to Akhenaten.

Amenhotep was the son of Thutmose IV and his minor wife Mutemwiya, born probably around 1401 BC. He later commissioned a depiction of his divine birth at Luxor Temple, claiming that his true father was the god Amun. In his second regnal year, Amenhotep married Tiye, daughter of Yuya and Thuya, who remained his Great Royal Wife throughout his reign. With Tiye he fathered at least two sons — Crown Prince Thutmose and Amenhotep IV — as well as daughters Sitamun, Henuttaneb, Iset, Nebetah, and Beketaten. Amenhotep contracted several diplomatic marriages to foreign princesses, including Gilukhepa of Mitanni, who arrived with a retinue of 317 women, and Tadukhepa of Mitanni around his 36th regnal year.

Amenhotep probably became pharaoh between the ages of six and twelve. He is known to have participated in only one military campaign: in his fifth regnal year he led a victorious expedition against a rebellion in Kush, commemorated by three rock-carved stelae found near Aswan and Sai in Nubia.

Amenhotep celebrated three Sed festivals in regnal years 30, 34, and 37, each held at his Malkata palace in Western Thebes. The Sed festival was an ancient tradition dating to the Old Kingdom, consisting of ritual tests demonstrating the pharaoh's fitness to continue ruling. Amenhotep wished his festivals to be the most spectacular Egypt had ever seen, appointing Amenhotep son of Hapu to plan the ceremonies. After the Sed festival, the king was considered to have transcended from near-god to fully divine.

The Amarna Letters, diplomatic documents discovered near Amarna, preserve foreign correspondence from Amenhotep's reign. Letters from the rulers of Assyria, Mitanni, Babylon, Hatti, and other states typically requested gold and luxury gifts from Egypt. Among the most significant is Amarna Letter EA 4, in which Amenhotep firmly refused to give his daughter in marriage to the Babylonian king — asserting both Egyptian royal custom and diplomatic superiority. He also sent an expedition to Cyprus in the 14th century BC to establish Egyptian control over the island.

Amenhotep's palace of Malkata, known as Per-Hay (House of Rejoicing), was built on the west bank of Thebes beginning around regnal year 11 and became his permanent residence around regnal year 29. Once completed it was the largest royal residence in Egypt, named Tjehen-Aten — the City of the Dazzling Sun Disk.

Amenhotep has the distinction of having more surviving statues than any other Egyptian pharaoh, with over 250 identified. His enormous mortuary temple on the west bank of the Nile was the largest religious complex in Thebes in its day, but collapsed within two centuries as it had been built too close to the floodplain. All that remained standing were the two colossal gateway statues known as the Colossi of Memnon, depicting Amenhotep at 18 metres in height. In 2014, two additional giant statues toppled by an earthquake around 1200 BC were reconstructed from over 200 fragments and re-erected at the northern gate of his funerary temple.

At Karnak, Amenhotep constructed the Third Pylon, erected 600 statues of the goddess Sekhmet, and created a forecourt decorated with scenes of the sacred barques of Amun, Mut, and Khonsu. He built extensively in Nubia at Elephantine, Wadi es-Sebuam, Aniba, Kawa, and Sesebi. In 1989, a spectacular cache of royal statues was discovered in the courtyard of his colonnade at Luxor Temple, including a near-perfect pink quartzite statue of the king wearing the Double Crown. In 2021, excavations revealed a settlement near his mortuary temple believed to have housed craftsmen and labourers working on his royal projects.

In his final years Amenhotep suffered from arthritis, obesity, and severe dental disease. A forensic examination of his mummy revealed worn and cavity-pitted teeth that must have caused constant pain. He died between the ages of 40 and 50. His mummy was discovered in 1898 in tomb KV35 and transferred to the National Museum of Egyptian Civilization in April 2021 as part of the Pharaohs' Golden Parade.

When Amenhotep died he left behind an internationally respected country at the height of its power, though one bound to age-old religious certainties under the Amun priesthood. The upheavals unleashed by his son Akhenaten's religious revolution shook these certainties to their foundations and posed the defining question of whether a pharaoh was more powerful than his society.

Babylon Fortress.detailed_knowledge:
Babylon Fortress is an ancient Roman fortress on the eastern bank of the Nile Delta, located in the area known today as Old Cairo or Coptic Cairo. The fortress was built circa 300 AD by Emperor Diocletian in order to protect the entrance to an ancient canal that linked the Nile with the Red Sea. It stood at the boundary between Lower and Middle Egypt, where river craft paid tolls when ascending or descending the Nile. Within the fortress's former enclosure are the present-day Coptic Museum, a convent, and several churches, including the Church of St. George and the Hanging Church.

According to the 7th-century writer John of Nikiu, the name "Babylon" was given by Nebuchadnezzar II, the second king of the Neo-Babylonian Empire, who campaigned in the area circa 568 BC and named it after his own home city of Babylon in Mesopotamia. According to Egyptologists, the ancient Egyptian name of the site was Kheriaha.

Some historical sources report that a fortress named Babylon was first founded by Nebuchadnezzar II circa 568 BC, at the site where an ancient Egyptian canal linked the Nile with the Red Sea. During an uprising, Babylonian prisoners established a stronghold between Memphis and Heliopolis on the east bank of the Nile. The canal was subsequently re-dug by the Persian king Darius (r. 521-486 BC).

The fortress's origins are more commonly associated with the Emperor Trajan (r. 98-117), who reconstructed the Red Sea canal — known as the Amnis Traianus — which had fallen out of use, moving its mouth south to the site of what is now Old Cairo and constructing a fortified harbour there. The construction of the present Babylon Fortress, whose remains are visible today, has been attributed by more recent archaeological research to the reign of Diocletian (r. 285-305), who expanded the fortifications at the mouth of the canal around 300 AD. The large new fortress, made necessary in part by the instability of the Crisis of the Third Century, provided considerable defence for both the land and sea routes of the region. The canal passed between two massive round towers and ran through the middle of the fortress; in later centuries a wall was built between the two towers to block the canal entirely.

The town was the seat of a Christian bishopric, a suffragan of Leontopolis, the capital of the Roman province of Augustamnica Secunda. After the Council of Chalcedon in 451, the community was divided between those who accepted the council's decrees and the majority who rejected them. Babylon is today listed by both the Eastern Orthodox Church and the Catholic Church as a titular see. During the Eastern Roman Empire period, the city revolted against the rule of Emperor Phocas. During the Arab conquest of Egypt, the Byzantine fortress held out for approximately seven months before finally falling in December 640 to the Arab general Amr ibn al-As. The history of this conquest is recorded by John, Bishop of Nikiu, in his Chronicle — a work that survives today only in Ethiopic manuscripts.

Within the area once enclosed by the ancient fortress walls lie some of the most important Christian monuments in Egypt. The Coptic Museum, founded in 1908, houses the world's largest collection of Coptic art and artefacts. The Hanging Church, one of the oldest and most celebrated churches in Egypt, is built directly over the southern gatehouse of the Roman fortress, its nave suspended above the ancient passageway — giving rise to its distinctive name. The Church of St. George and several other early Christian foundations also stand within or immediately adjacent to the fortress precinct, making Old Cairo one of the most historically layered religious sites in the country.

Bent pyramid for senefru.detailed_knowledge:
The Bent Pyramid is an ancient Egyptian pyramid located at the royal necropolis of Dahshur, approximately 40 kilometres south of Cairo, built under the Old Kingdom Pharaoh Sneferu. A unique example of early pyramid development in Egypt, it was the second of four pyramids built by Sneferu. The Bent Pyramid rises from the desert at a 54-degree inclination, but the top section is built at the shallower angle of 43 degrees, lending the pyramid its visibly "bent" appearance. It is unique among the approximately 118 pyramids found in Egypt in that its original polished limestone outer casing remains largely intact — the only classical pyramid for which this is true. The ancient formal name of the Bent Pyramid is generally translated as "The Southern Shining Pyramid," or "Sneferu is Shining in the South."

The Bent Pyramid represents a crucial transitional moment in Egyptian architecture, marking the shift from step-sided pyramids to smooth-sided ones. It has long been suggested that the original angle of inclination proved too steep during construction, forcing the builders to adopt a shallower angle to avert structural collapse — a theory supported by the fact that the adjacent Red Pyramid, built immediately afterwards by Sneferu, was constructed at the reduced angle of 43 degrees from its base. In 1974, Kurt Mendelssohn proposed that the change in angle was a stability precaution taken in response to a catastrophic collapse of the Meidum Pyramid. More recent scholarship suggests a geometric explanation: ramp construction became increasingly impractical at inclinations above 52 degrees, making steeper angles effectively impossible to complete. The experiments with different gradients at Dahshur document the builders' search for the optimal solution — a process that made the eventual perfection of the Giza pyramids possible.

The Bent Pyramid was the second of three pyramids constructed by Sneferu during his long reign, all reflecting an experimental phase of the pyramid programme whose lessons enabled Sneferu's son Khufu to achieve the architectural mastery of the Great Pyramid. The smooth-sided form is interpreted by some scholars as a symbol of the solar cult and of the primeval mound from which all life sprang, rather than a staircase to the stars.

The Bent Pyramid underwent three distinct construction phases. In the first phase, a pyramid with a base of 157 metres and an inclination of approximately 58 degrees was planned. In the second phase, the design was extended with a reduced angle of 54 degrees and a base increased to 188 metres, faced in fine Tura limestone. In the third and final phase, the angle was reduced to 43 degrees and the masonry laid in horizontal layers — a method adopted in all subsequent Egyptian pyramids. The slope reduction created the distinctive kink visible today and reduced the total height to 105 metres, with a total volume of approximately 1,440,808 cubic metres.

The Bent Pyramid has two entrances connected to two separate chamber systems. The northern entrance leads via a 74-metre descending passage to a partly subterranean antechamber 12.60 metres in height with a corbelled limestone roof. A short corridor leads to the burial chamber measuring 4.96 by 4.30 metres. The western entrance leads down a 67.66-metre passage to the upper burial chamber measuring 7.97 by 5.26 metres with a four-sided corbelled roof of 16.50 metres. The upper chamber was left near-finished, with cracks covered in gypsum. One block bears the hieroglyphs of Sneferu, confirming the pyramid's attribution to him. No sarcophagus was found in either chamber. Both chambers feature rows of original cedar beams, interpreted by most scholars as part of the funerary support structure.

The valley temple of the Bent Pyramid measures 26.20 by 47.16 metres and is divided into three sections — two covered and one open courtyard. A causeway approximately 700 metres in length, paved with limestone blocks and flanked by low walls, leads from the pyramid's northeast corner toward the valley temple. The design of these open causeways, which allowed sand to drift and block access, later prompted builders to construct fully enclosed causeways for subsequent pyramid complexes.

A satellite pyramid is located 55 metres south of the Bent Pyramid, originally measuring 26 metres in height with faces inclining at 44 degrees 30 minutes. Its burial chamber has a corbelled vault ceiling and contains a shaft probably dug by treasure hunters. Like the main pyramid, the satellite had its own altar with two stelae on the eastern side.

German archaeologists Nicole Alexanian and Felix Arnold demonstrated that the Bent Pyramid sits within a deliberately designed landscape. Unlike all other Egyptian pyramids, which were built near fertile land along the Nile, the Bent Pyramid occupies a pristine desert plateau that appears to have been artificially levelled, with ancient garden enclosures, water basins, and a wadi channel connecting the pyramid to a harbour. Alexanian and Arnold describe the result concisely as "an artificial mountain erected within an artificial landscape."

In July 2019, Egypt opened the Bent Pyramid to tourism for the first time since 1965, allowing visitors to traverse a 79-metre narrow tunnel to reach two chambers dating back approximately 4,600 years. The adjacent satellite pyramid was also opened to the public for the first time since its excavation in 1956.

Cairo Citadel.detailed_knowledge:
The Citadel of Cairo, also known as the Citadel of Saladin, is a medieval Islamic-era fortification in Cairo, Egypt. Its construction was begun by Salah ad-Din (Saladin) in 1176 and continued by subsequent Egyptian rulers. It was the seat of government in Egypt and the residence of its rulers for nearly 700 years, from the 13th century until the construction of Abdeen Palace in the 19th century. Its location on a promontory of the Mokattam Hills commands a strategic position overlooking the city and dominating its skyline. In 1979, UNESCO proclaimed it part of the World Heritage Site Historic Cairo, describing the area as "the new centre of the Islamic world, reaching its golden age in the 14th century."

The Citadel was built by the Kurdish Ayyubid ruler Saladin between 1176 and 1183 CE to protect Cairo from potential Crusader attacks and to provide a secure seat of government. Saladin envisioned a great wall approximately 20 kilometres long surrounding both Cairo and the former capital Fustat, with the Citadel as its centrepiece. He charged his chief eunuch Baha al-Din Qaraqush with overseeing the construction. Most of the structure was built with limestone quarried from the Mokattam Hills, though materials were also taken from minor pyramids at Giza and Abusir, and labour was provided by Christian prisoners of war captured in Saladin's campaigns against the Crusaders.

Construction continued under Sultan al-Adil (r. 1200-1218) and was largely completed under Sultan al-Kamil (1218-1238). Al-Kamil built or completed the palaces in the southern section and became the first ruler to move his residence to the Citadel in 1206. He also established a hippodrome to the west and south for equestrian training and military ceremonies, and completed the first aqueduct bringing Nile water to the fortress.

Only under the Mamluks, who ruled from 1250 to 1517, did the Citadel finally become the permanent residence of the sultans. The general layout divides it into two main enclosures: the Northern Enclosure, historically reserved for military garrisons, and the Southern Enclosure, developed as the sultan's residence and administrative centre. This functional division was established by Sultan Baybars (r. 1260-1277), who built the Bab al-Qulla gate separating the two enclosures and developed the Southern Enclosure into an exclusive royal complex with a harem, audience halls, and ceremonial spaces.

The greatest builder of the Mamluk period was Sultan al-Nasir Muhammad, sultan three times over nearly fifty years between 1293 and 1341. Among his most important constructions was the Ablaq Palace (the Striped Palace), built in 1313-1314 and named for its distinctive red-and-black stonework. He also built the Great Iwan, the sultan's public ceremonial throne room, frequently cited by chroniclers as the most impressive structure in Cairo. His mosque, built in 1318 and renovated in 1335, is the only major structure of his reign still preserved at the Citadel. Most of its rich marble decoration was stripped away and shipped to Istanbul by the Ottoman Sultan Selim I after his conquest of Egypt.

To supply water to the Citadel, Saladin built an 85-metre-deep well known as the Well of Joseph (Bir Yusuf), named after his Arabic birth name Yusuf. Its shaft is cut from solid rock and surrounded by a long spiral staircase — earning it the alternative name the Spiral Well. Oxen powered waterwheels to raise water to the surface. During al-Nasir Muhammad's reign, this well was supplemented by a new aqueduct system raising Nile water along raised channels to the base of the Citadel.

Egypt was conquered by the Ottoman Empire in 1517. Under Ottoman rule the Citadel was divided into sections housing different military corps, and many Mamluk structures fell into ruin or were stripped of their decoration. The Mosque of Sulayman Pasha, built in 1528 for the Janissary garrison, is one of the few mosques in Cairo reflecting the classical Ottoman architectural style.

Muhammad Ali Pasha, appointed by the Ottoman sultan in 1805 to restore order after the French occupation, effectively established himself as Egypt's independent ruler. In 1811 he consolidated power through a violent coup in which he invited the remaining Mamluk leaders to a banquet at the Citadel and had them massacred as they departed through Bab al-Azab. Between 1828 and 1848, Muhammad Ali built the mosque that bears his name atop the Citadel — an Ottoman-style structure with a large dome and tall pencil minarets that remains one of the most prominent monuments on Cairo's skyline. To make way for the mosque, he demolished many remaining Mamluk structures including the Great Iwan and the Ablaq Palace. Muhammad Ali was eventually buried in his mosque's courtyard.

The Citadel ceased to function as the seat of Egyptian government in 1874 when Khedive Ismail moved to the newly built Abdin Palace in downtown Cairo. For much of the 20th century it served as a military garrison — first for the British, then for the Egyptian Army — until it was opened to the public in 1983. Today the Citadel is a major tourist destination housing four historic mosques and several museums, including the Egyptian Military Museum, the al-Gawhara Palace Museum, the Carriage Museum, and the Police Museum.

Cairo Tower.detailed_knowledge:
The Cairo Tower is a free-standing concrete tower in Cairo, Egypt. At 187 metres (614 ft), it was the tallest structure in Egypt for 37 years until 1998, and the tallest structure in North Africa for 21 years until 1982. It stands in the Gezira district on Gezira Island in the River Nile, close to downtown Cairo, and is considered one of Egypt's most recognisable modern landmarks — sometimes described as the country's second most famous monument after the Pyramids of Giza.

Built between 1956 and 1961, the tower was designed by the Egyptian architect Naoum Shebib, who drew inspiration from the forms of ancient Egyptian architecture. Its partially open lattice-work exterior is intended to evoke a pharaonic lotus plant — one of the most iconic symbols of ancient Egypt — and the tower is crowned by a circular observation deck and a revolving restaurant offering panoramic views across greater Cairo.

According to documents published by Major General Adel Shaheen, the funds for the tower's construction originated from the United States government through the CIA, which had provided approximately one to three million US dollars to President Gamal Abdel Nasser as a personal gift with the intent of discouraging his support for the Algerian Revolution and other African independence movements. Affronted by what he regarded as an attempt at bribery, Nasser transferred the entire sum to the Egyptian government and directed it toward the tower's construction — declaring that the monument would be visible from the US Embassy across the Nile as a symbol of Egypt's, Africa's, and the Arab world's resistance and independence. The tower thus carries a charged political history from its very origins, embodying the spirit of Egyptian sovereignty during the Nasserist era.

The tower's unconventional origins gave rise to sardonic nicknames both in Egypt and abroad. Egyptians began calling it the "waqf Roosevelt" (Roosevelt's endowment), a phrase that was mistakenly rendered in translation as something rather more provocative, prompting an equally irreverent American response. Despite these controversies — including a fatwa issued by a local religious group in the 1990s calling for its demolition — the tower retained its status as a beloved national landmark and popular tourist destination.

Between 2006 and 2009, the tower underwent a comprehensive restoration carried out by the Egyptian Arab Contractors Company at a cost of approximately 15 million Egyptian pounds. The restoration included treating and reinforcing the tower's concrete structure, adding three floors of metal construction at the base, a new floor above the entrance, an emergency staircase, an upgraded visitor elevator, and new LED lighting throughout.

The tower rises from Gezira Island — one of the most affluent and culturally significant districts of Cairo — situated between the eastern and western branches of the Nile. The island is home to the Cairo Opera House, the Gezira Sporting Club, and several embassies and cultural institutions, making it one of the most cosmopolitan areas of the Egyptian capital. From the observation deck at the top of the Cairo Tower, visitors can take in an unobstructed 360-degree panorama encompassing the Pyramids of Giza to the southwest, the minarets and domes of Islamic Cairo to the east, the green expanse of the Nile's banks, and the sprawling modern city stretching in every direction to the horizon. The tower remains one of the most visited attractions in Cairo and a defining feature of the capital's contemporary skyline.

Colossoi of Memnon.detailed_knowledge:
The Colossi of Memnon (Arabic: el-Colossat or es-Salamat) are two large stone statues of the Pharaoh Amenhotep III, which stand at the front of the ruined Mortuary Temple of Amenhotep III, the largest temple in the Theban Necropolis. They have stood since 1350 BC, and were well known to ancient Greeks and Romans, as well as early modern travelers and Egyptologists. The statues contain 107 Roman-era inscriptions in Greek and Latin, dated to between AD 20 and 250; many of these inscriptions on the northernmost statue make reference to the Greek mythological king Memnon, whom the statue was then erroneously thought to represent. Scholars have debated how the identification of the northern colossus as "Memnon" is connected to the Greek name for the entire Theban Necropolis as the Memnonium.

Description The twin statues depict Amenhotep III (fl. 14th century BC) in a seated position, his hands resting on his knees and his gaze facing eastwards (actually ESE in modern bearings) towards the river. Three shorter figures are carved into the front throne alongside his legs: these are his wife Tiye and mother Mutemwiya. The middle between Amenhotep III's ankles would have been an unidentified daughter, although it has been destroyed. The side panels depict the Nile god Hapi.

The statues are made from blocks of quartzite sandstone which was quarried at el-Gabal el-Ahmar (near modern-day Cairo) and transported 675 km (420 mi) overland to Thebes (Luxor). The stones are believed to be too heavy to have been transported upstream on the Nile. The blocks used by later Roman engineers to reconstruct the northern colossus may have come from Edfu (north of Aswan). Including the stone platforms on which they stand – themselves about 4 m (13 ft) – the colossi reach 18 m (60 ft) in height and weigh an estimated 720 tons each. The two figures are about 15 m (50 ft) apart. The size of the statues makes them visible up to over 10 miles away. Regarding their size; they stood as the guardians of Amenhotep III's mortuary temple. Amenhotep III's process of immortalizing himself in statuary was prevalent, His approach to statuary representation and inscriptions had supported his ideology as being much more than the king of kings. His titles repeatedly called him the "good god". Suzanne Bickel describes the height of both colossi were accessible to the surrounding public as a method of allowing all to worship Amenhotep III himself and the gods at his temple. Both statues are quite damaged, with the features above the waist virtually unrecognizable. The southern statue comprises a single piece of stone, but the northern figure has a large extensive crack in the lower half and above the waist consists of 5 tiers of stone. These upper levels consist of a different type of sandstone, and are the result of a later reconstruction attempt, which William de Wiveleslie Abney attributed to Septimius Severus. It is believed that originally the two statues were identical to each other, although inscriptions and minor art may have varied. The original function of the Colossi was to stand guard at the entrance to Amenhotep's memorial temple (or mortuary temple): a massive construct built during the pharaoh's lifetime, where he was worshipped as a god-on-earth both before and after his departure from this world. In its day, this temple complex was the largest and most opulent in Ancient Egypt. Covering a total of 35 hectares (86 acres), even later rivals such as Ramesses II's Ramesseum or Ramesses III's Medinet Habu were unable to match it in area; even the Temple of Karnak, as it stood in Amenhotep's time, was smaller.

Floods Amenhotep III had positioned the mortuary temple in front of the floodplain of the Nile in an effort to fill a lake in front of the Colossi. Furthermore, this lake acted as a water retention reservoir and prevented the temple from flooding completely during high inundations. Hourig Sourouzian felt that Amenhotep III did not plan for the site to flood altogether as the surrounding wall for the mortuary temple behind the Colossi was constructed primarily of mud brick. With the exception of the Colossi, however, very little remains today of Amenhotep's temple. It stood on the edge of the Nile floodplain, and successive annual inundations gnawed away at its foundations – a 1840s lithograph by David Roberts shows the Colossi surrounded by water – and it was not unknown for later rulers to dismantle, purloin, and reuse portions of their predecessors' monuments.

Roman era inscriptions The statues contain 107 Roman-era inscriptions in Greek and Latin, dated between 20-250CE; these inscriptions allowed modern travellers to connect the statues to the classical Greek and Latin literature. Many of the inscriptions include the name "Memnon". Several of the inscriptions refer to sounds emitted by the statues, the most famous being poetry by Julia Balbilla recording multiple visits during November 130 CE in a party that included the Emperor Hadrian and his wife Vibia Sabina. Of the 107 Roman-era inscriptions: 61 of them are written in Greek, 45 are written in Latin, and one bilingual text is written. They were first studied in detail by Jean-Antoine Letronne in his 1831 La statue vocale de Memnon considérée dans ses rapports avec l'Égypte et la Grèce and then catalogued in the second volume (1848) of his Recueil des inscriptions grecques et latines de l'Égypte.

Earthquakes Soon after its construction the temple was destroyed by an earthquake, recently dated by the Armenian Institute of Seismology to around 1200 BC, which left only the two huge colossi at the entrance still standing. These were further destroyed by an earthquake in 27 BC, after which they were partly reconstructed by the Roman authorities. The 1200 BC earthquake also opened numerous chasms in the ground which meant that many statues were buried, some in pristine condition. These have been the subject of extensive restoration and excavation conducted by the Armenian/German archaeologist Hourig Sourouzian, who has revealed that the complex consisted of three pylons, each fronted by colossal statues, while at the far end a rectangular Temple complex consisted of a peristyle court surrounded by columns. So far four of the statues have been re-erected, with eight waiting to be re-erected, while some 200 statues or pieces of statues are in the Luxor Museum, some on display, others in store awaiting conservation.

Name The modern Arabic name is Kom el-Hatan, but it is generally known by the Roman name as the Temple of Memnon. Memnon was a hero of the Trojan War and King of Ethiopia, who led his armies from Africa into Asia Minor to help defend the beleaguered city of Troy but was ultimately slain by Achilles. Memnon (whose name means the Steadfast or Resolute) was said to be the son of Eos, the goddess of dawn. He was associated with colossi built several centuries earlier, because of the reported cry at dawn of the northern statue (see below), which became known as the Colossus of Memnon. Eventually, the entire Theban Necropolis became generally referred to as the Memnonium making him "Ruler of the west" as in the case of the god Osiris who was called chief of the west. In the 19th century, William de Wiveleslie Abney noted that " t he Arabs called these statues 'Shama' and 'Tama', and when speaking of them together gave them the appelation of Sanamât, or the idols."

Sounds In 27 BCE, a large earthquake reportedly shattered the northern colossus, collapsing it from the waist up and cracking the lower half. Following its rupture, the remaining lower half of this statue was then reputed to "sing" on various occasions – always within an hour or two of sunrise, usually right at dawn. The sound was most often reported in February or March, but this is probably more a reflection of the tourist season rather than any actual pattern. The earliest report in literature is that of the Greek historian and geographer Strabo, who said that he heard the sound during a visit in 20 BCE, by which time it apparently was already well-known. The description varied; Strabo said it sounded "like a blow", Pausanias compared it to "the string of a lyre" breaking, but it also was described as the striking of brass or whistling. Other ancient sources include Pliny (not from personal experience, but he collected other reports), Tacitus, Philostratus and Juvenal. In addition, the base of the statue is inscribed with about 90 surviving inscriptions of contemporary tourists reporting whether they had heard the sound or not. The legend of the "Vocal Memnon", the luck that hearing it was reputed to bring, and the reputation of the statue's oracular powers became known outside of Egypt, and a constant stream of visitors, including several Roman emperors, came to marvel at the statues. The last recorded reliable mention of the sound dates from 196. Sometime later in the Roman era, the upper tiers of sandstone were added (the original remains of the top half have never been found); the date of this reconstruction is unknown, but local tradition places it circa 199, and attributes it to the Roman Emperor Septimius Severus in an attempt to curry favour with the oracle (it is known that he visited the statue, but did not hear the sound). Various explanations have been offered for the phenomenon; these are of two types: natural or man-made. Strabo himself apparently was too far away to be able to determine its nature: he reported that he could not determine if it came from the pedestal, the shattered upper area, or "the people standing around at the base". If natural, the sound was probably caused by rising temperatures and the evaporation of dew inside the porous rock. Similar sounds, although much rarer, have been heard from some of the other Egyptian monuments (Karnak is the usual location for more modern reports). Perhaps the most convincing argument against it being the result of human agents is that it did cease, probably due to the added weight of the reconstructed upper tiers. Since the northernmost statue is commonly associated with the renowned acoustic phenomenon, much of the literature and cultural discourse tends to overlook the southern counterpart. Despite its significance as a key structure commissioned by Amenhotep III, this oversight persisted. As a result, many Greco-Roman travelers, drawn by the mystique of the site, would etch their names upon the colossi during their visits, leaving behind inscriptions that reflect their fascination and desire for posterity.

Dahshur Pyramids.detailed_knowledge:
Dahshur  is an ancient Egyptian pyramid complex and necropolis and shares the name of the nearby village of Manshiyyat Dahshur (Arabic: منشأة دهشور) in markaz Badrashin, Giza. Dahshur is listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site, and is located on the Western Desert plateau at the edge of the cultivated plain, and along with the pyramid complexes at Saqqara, Abusir, and Giza, to its north, forms the pyramid fields of the ancient capital city of Memphis. It is known chiefly for several pyramids, mainly Sneferu's Bent Pyramid and the Red Pyramid, which are among the oldest, largest and best preserved in Egypt, built from 2613 to 2589 BC.

Pyramids The Dahshur pyramids were an extremely important learning experience for the Egyptians. It provided them with the knowledge and know-how to transition from step-sided pyramids to smooth-sided pyramids. Ultimately their breadth of experience would allow them to build the Great Pyramid of Giza; the last of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World still standing to this date. The first of the Dahshur pyramids was the Bent Pyramid (2613–2589 BC), built under the rule of King Sneferu. The Bent Pyramid was the first attempt at building a smooth sided pyramid, but proved to be an unsuccessful build due to the miscalculations made on the structural weight that was being placed onto the soft ground (sand, gravel, and clay), which had a tendency to subside. Other calculations that were proven to be erroneous were that the blocks being used were cut in such a manner that when placed onto the pyramid their weight was not distributed appropriately, causing the angle of the pyramid to be off and achieving the name "the Bent Pyramid". Realizing his shortcomings and learning from his mistakes, King Sneferu ordered the building of the second pyramid of Dahshur, the Red Pyramid. Once completed, the pyramid was considered to be a success, as it was a fully constructed, smooth sided, and a free standing pyramid rising to a height of 341 feet (104 meters), with an angle of 43 degrees. The Red Pyramid's name derives from the material that was used to construct the pyramid, red limestone. This pyramid is believed to be the resting place of King Sneferu.

Another pyramid, the White Pyramid, located within Dahshur is that of the 12th Dynasty King Amenemhat II (1929–1895 BC). This pyramid has not been preserved as well as the others within the area due to the materials that were used to fill it (sand on the outside and limestone on the inside). The weather caused the sand to erode from it, but the limestone was taken intentionally for use on other pyramids allowing the pyramid to collapse and ultimately desecrating the tomb of King Amenemhat II. King Senusret III (1878–1839 BC) had his pyramid built within Dahshur. The difference between his pyramid in comparison to those surrounding it was that King Senusret III had tombs and galleries built underneath it for two princesses; Sit-Hathor and Merit. The Black Pyramid dates from the later reign of Amenemhat III and, although badly eroded, it remains the most imposing monument at the site after the two Sneferu pyramids. The polished granite pyramidion or capstone of the Black Pyramid is on display in the main hall of the Egyptian Museum in Cairo.

Several other pyramids of the 13th Dynasty were built at Dahshur. Only the Pyramid of Ameny Qemau has been excavated so far by Ahmad Fakhri, the archaeologist who excavated this site.

Tombs and cemeteries Located closely to the pyramid of the 12th Dynasty several undisturbed tombs of royal women were found, containing a large amount of lapidary and jewelry that have been determined to be of the highest stage of metalworking in Egypt during this time period. The pyramid of Senusret III was part of a huge complex, with several smaller pyramids of royal women, along with another pyramid to the south. In a gallery tomb next to this pyramid were found two treasures of the king's daughters (Sithathor). Extensive cemeteries of officials of the Old Kingdom and Middle Kingdom have been found around Dahshur's pyramids. Dahshur was Egypt's royal necropolis during the reign of the 12th Dynasty king Amenemhat II.

Contemporary history In July 2012, Dahshur's entire Christian community, which some estimate to be as many as 120 families, fled to nearby towns due to sectarian violence. The violence began in a dispute over a badly ironed shirt, which in turn escalated into a fight in which a Christian burned a Muslim Arab clan member to death. Furthermore, during clashes another Muslim suffered head injuries and later died due to a gasoline bomb being thrown from a rooftop of a building. At least 16 homes and properties of Christians were pillaged, some were torched, and a church was damaged during the violence. This incident was reported internationally. As of January 2013, and due to the security vacuum that still prevails in Egypt following the 2011 uprising, the site is under threat of desecration and damage due to encroachment by locals of surrounding urban settlements.

Climate Dahshur has a hot desert climate (BWh) according to the Köppen-Geiger climate classification system.

See also Memphite Necropolis List of ancient Egyptian towns and cities List of ancient Egyptian sites, including sites of temples List of megalithic sites Acanthus (Egypt), an old village in Dahshur mentioned in Ancient Greek literature.

Deir el-Bahari.location: West Bank of Luxor, Luxor Governorate, Egypt
Deir el-Bahari.detailed_knowledge:
Deir el-Bahari is a complex of mortuary temples and tombs located on the west bank of the Nile, opposite the city of Luxor, Egypt, forming part of the Theban Necropolis. Its history spans more than two millennia, beginning with the Eleventh Dynasty and continuing through the Ptolemaic and Roman periods. The site is dominated by three mortuary temples — those of Mentuhotep II, Hatshepsut, and Thutmose III — and contains one of the most celebrated royal mummy caches ever discovered.

The site's history begins in the Eleventh Dynasty, when Pharaoh Mentuhotep II (c. 2061-2010 BC) constructed his funerary temple here to commemorate the reunification of Egypt after the First Intermediate Period. His terraced complex, integrating a royal tomb into a temple platform and carved into the natural cliffs, pioneered the style of cliffside temple construction later emulated by New Kingdom rulers. Centuries later, the Eighteenth Dynasty queen Hatshepsut erected her famous temple, Djeser-Djeseru, directly beside Mentuhotep's complex. Designed by her architect Senenmut, it was richly decorated with reliefs celebrating her divine birth, a commercial expedition to the land of Punt, and her association with Amun-Ra. Later still, Thutmose III added a smaller temple, Djeser-Akhet, above hers. By the Third Intermediate Period, Deir el-Bahari had become a burial ground for priests and a hiding place for royal mummies, including those discovered in the DB320 cache in 1881.

Archaeological exploration intensified in the late 19th century. Edouard Naville's excavations from 1893 to 1906 revealed both Hatshepsut's temple and Mentuhotep II's complex. Herbert Winlock's Metropolitan Museum of Art expeditions (1911-1931) unearthed significant finds including statues of Hatshepsut and Thutmose III. The Polish Centre of Mediterranean Archaeology has led extensive restoration work since the 1960s. During the Ptolemaic period, parts of Hatshepsut's temple were reused for the cults of Imhotep and Amenhotep, Son of Hapu.

Mentuhotep II's funerary complex at Deir el-Bahari is one of the most unusual in Egypt. Built on several levels within the great bay in the cliffs, it was approached by a 16-metre-wide causeway from a valley temple that no longer survives. The mortuary temple consists of a forecourt, an entrance gate, and a terrace supporting a large square structure that may represent the primeval mound arising from the waters of chaos. An underground passage known as the Bab el-Hosan (Gate of the Horseman) leads from the eastern forecourt to an unfinished tomb containing a seated statue of the king. The inner part of the temple was cut into the cliff and consists of a peristyle court, a hypostyle hall, and a passage leading to the royal tomb. The mastaba-like structure on the terrace is surrounded by a pillared ambulatory where shrines and tombs of several royal wives and daughters — priestesses of Hathor — were found. Six sarcophagi were retrieved from the tombs of the royal ladies Ashayet, Henhenet, Kawit, Kemsit, Muyet, and Sadhe; the sarcophagus of Queen Kawit, now in the Cairo Museum, is particularly fine.

The focal point of Deir el-Bahari is Djeser-Djeseru, meaning the Holy of Holies — the Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut. Designed and built by Senenmut, it was intended for Hatshepsut's posthumous worship and to honour the glory of Amun. The temple sits atop a series of three colonnaded terraces reaching 30 metres in height, connected by long ramps that once were graced with gardens, and built directly into the cliff face behind it. The relief sculpture within recites the tale of the divine birth of the pharaoh, tells of an expedition to the Land of Punt on the Red Sea coast, and depicts Hatshepsut offering to Horus. The temple once featured two colossal statues of Osiris, a long avenue lined by sphinxes, and numerous sculptures of the queen — most of which were destroyed or defaced following Hatshepsut's posthumous condemnation, and others rededicated to Thutmose III. During the Ptolemaic and Roman periods, the temple became a major cult site for the deified Amenhotep, Son of Hapu, and Imhotep. Conservation has been ongoing since the 19th century, with the Polish Centre of Mediterranean Archaeology leading major projects since the 1960s and modern 3D scanning technologies introduced in the 2000s.

The Mortuary Temple of Thutmose III, known as Djeser-Akhet (Holy of the Horizon), was built around 1435-1425 BC and rediscovered by a Polish-Egyptian mission in 1961-1962. It features a basilica-style hypostyle hall with clerestory windows — a rare architectural innovation that foreshadowed later Ramesside hypostyle halls. The temple was primarily dedicated to Amun-Ra and included a Hathor shrine housing a statue of the goddess as a cow protecting the king. It was heavily damaged by an 11th-century BC earthquake and subsequent stone-robbing, leaving only foundations and scattered architectural elements.

A tomb designated TT320 contained a cache of forty royal mummies moved there by Twenty-first Dynasty priests to prevent desecration. Among them were the mummies of Ahmose I, Amenhotep I, Thutmose I, Thutmose II, Thutmose III, Ramesses I, Seti I, Ramesses II, and Ramesses IX, as well as the high priests Pinedjem I and Pinedjem II. A separate cache of 153 priestly mummies was discovered in 1891 in the nearby tomb known as Bab el-Gasus. Private tombs include that of Meketre (TT280), which contained painted wooden funerary models and the first recorded human-headed canopic jar, and the unfinished tomb of Senenmut, whose corridors extend over 90 metres.

In March 2020, archaeologists from Warsaw University discovered a 3,500-year-old stone chest containing ritual offerings — bundles of flax, a sacrificed goose skeleton, goose eggs, and what is believed to be an ibis egg, all wrapped in linen canvas. A small wooden box found inside is believed to bear the name of Pharaoh Thutmose II.

Today, Deir el-Bahari is one of Egypt's most visited archaeological sites and part of the UNESCO World Heritage listing Ancient Thebes with its Necropolis. The site was the scene of the Luxor Massacre on 17 November 1997, when militants killed over 60 tourists at Hatshepsut's temple. Since then, extensive security measures have been implemented and tourism has largely recovered. Deir el-Bahari continues to serve as a focal point of scholarly research and a testament to Egypt's enduring ancient legacy.

Dendera Temple Complex.detailed_knowledge:
The Dendera Temple complex is located about 2.5 kilometres south-east of Dendera, Egypt. It is one of the best-preserved temple complexes of ancient Egypt, situated in what was the sixth nome of Upper Egypt, south of Abydos. The entirety of the complex is surrounded by a substantial mudbrick enclosure wall. Due to its massive size, the structures throughout the complex were constructed over many eras, including the Middle Kingdom, the Ptolemaic period, and the period of Roman provincial rule. There is evidence of an even earlier building on this site dating to around 2250 BC, which may have begun during the reign of Pepi I and been completed under his son, Merenre Nemtyemsaf I. Evidence also exists of a temple here in the Eighteenth Dynasty (c. 1500 BC). The earliest surviving building in the compound today is the mammisi (birth house) raised by Nectanebo II, the last of the native pharaohs (360-343 BC).

The complex includes the main Temple of Hathor, a Temple of the Birth of Isis, a sacred lake used as a source of water for sacred rituals and everyday purposes, a sanatorium where the sacred waters were used to bless inscriptions on statues in order to cure diseases and where visitors could stay overnight to receive healing dreams, two mammisi (birth houses), a barque shrine used as a resting place for divine statues during festival processions, and the gateways of the Roman emperors Domitian and Trajan.

The structure that dominates and defines the entire complex is the Temple of Hathor. The original temple underwent continuous modifications from the Middle Kingdom until the beginning of the reign of the Roman emperor Trajan. The existing temple's structure began construction in 54 BC during the late Ptolemaic period under Ptolemy XII Auletes. The great hypostyle hall was added in the Roman period under Tiberius. On the rear exterior wall of the temple is a celebrated carving of Cleopatra VII Philopator and her son by Julius Caesar, Ptolemy XV Caesarion. The temple's layout encompasses a large and small hypostyle hall, a laboratory, storage magazines, an offering hall, a Hall of the Ennead, a great sanctuary, and numerous subsidiary shrines dedicated to deities including Isis, Sokar, Harsomtus, and Hathor in her various aspects. The crypts beneath the temple consist of twelve subterranean chambers used for storing sacred vessels and divine iconography, containing reliefs dated as late as the reign of Ptolemy XII.

One of the most celebrated objects ever discovered at the complex is the Dendera Zodiac, a sculptured relief found in a late Greco-Roman chapel on the roof of the Temple of Hathor. It depicts the zodiac system as known in the Greco-Roman world, including recognisable signs such as Taurus the bull and Libra the scales, set within a scene of the Egyptian sky populated by gods and constellations. A sketch was made during the Napoleonic campaign in Egypt. In 1820 it was removed from the temple ceiling by French agents and replaced with a plaster copy; the original is now in the Louvre in Paris. Jean-Francois Champollion, the decipherer of the Rosetta Stone, dated it to the Ptolemaic period, and Egyptologists now place it more precisely in the first century BC.

The Hathor Temple also contains a series of stone reliefs known popularly as the Dendera light. They depict Harsomtus — a form of Horus understood as a primeval creator and either Hathor's son or lover — in the form of a snake emerging from a lotus flower and positioned within an oval container that may represent the womb of the sky goddess Nut. Six such reliefs exist; their resemblance to a modern light bulb has attracted popular speculation, though Egyptologists interpret them as standard Egyptian religious symbolism.

A processional stairway leads to the roof of the temple, its walls decorated with reliefs depicting rituals performed during the New Year festival, when divine images were carried to the roof to be revitalised by the rays of the rising sun. The staircase shows considerable wear from millennia of use and has accumulated an apparent accretion of material, earning it the informal name of the melted stairs.

The Roman mammisi is a subsidiary birth house dating to the reigns of Trajan and Marcus Aurelius, with numerous reliefs depicting Trajan making offerings to Egyptian deities while dressed as a pharaoh — a vivid illustration of Rome's relationship with Egyptian religious tradition.

In March 2023, archaeologists excavating at the site uncovered a limestone sphinx with a slight grin and dimples, believed to have been created in the image of the Roman emperor Claudius, wearing a nemes headdress surmounted by a cobra-shaped uraeus. The Supreme Council of Antiquities began a major restoration project at the complex in 2005, resumed in 2017 after the completion of scientific studies.
