Her legacy marks the end of the Twelfth Dynasty and transition into the Second Intermediate Period, and she remains the first clearly attested female pharaoh in Egyptian history.

Sphinx.detailed_knowledge:
The Great Sphinx of Giza is a limestone statue of a reclining sphinx, a mythical creature with the head of a human and the body of a lion. The monument was sculpted from the limestone bedrock of the Eocene-aged Mokattam Formation and faces east on the Giza Plateau, on the west bank of the Nile in Giza, Egypt. The oldest known monumental sculpture in Egypt, the Sphinx is part of the Memphite Necropolis and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

Archaeological evidence suggests the Sphinx was created by Egyptians of the Old Kingdom during the reign of Khufu (c. 2590-2566 BC) or Khafre (c. 2558-2532 BC). Scholars and Egyptologists believe the face of the Sphinx was carved to represent either the pharaoh Khufu or one of his sons, pharaohs Djedefre and Khafre, but a consensus has not been reached and the person in whose likeness the Sphinx was carved remains in dispute. The monument is 73 m (240 ft) long from paw to tail, 20 m (66 ft) high from the base to the top of the head, and 19 m (62 ft) wide at its rear haunches.

The original name the Old Kingdom creators gave the Sphinx is unknown. In the New Kingdom, the Sphinx was revered as the solar deity Hor-em-akhet, meaning Horus of the Horizon, and the 14th century BC pharaoh Thutmose IV specifically refers to it as such in his Dream Stele. The commonly used name Sphinx was given to it in classical antiquity, around 2,000 years after its construction. The medieval Egyptian Arabic name is Abu il-Hawl, meaning father of terror.

The archaeological evidence suggests the Great Sphinx was created between 2600 and 2500 BC for the king Khufu, the builder of the Great Pyramid of Giza, or his son Khafre. The Sphinx is a monolith carved from the bedrock of the plateau, which also served as the quarry for the pyramids and other monuments in the area. Egyptian geologist Farouk El-Baz has suggested the head of the Sphinx may have been carved first, out of a natural yardang — a ridge of bedrock sculpted by the wind. The stones cut from around the Sphinx's body were used to construct a temple in front of it; however, neither the enclosure nor the temple were completed.

Some time around the First Intermediate Period, the Giza Necropolis was abandoned and drifting sand eventually buried the Sphinx up to its shoulders. The first documented attempt at excavation dates to around 1400 BC, when the young Thutmose IV managed to dig out the front paws, between which he erected a shrine housing the Dream Stele — an inscribed granite slab possibly repurposed from one of Khafre's temples. In the New Kingdom, the Sphinx became more specifically associated with the sun god Hor-em-akhet. The Pharaoh Amenhotep II built a temple to the northeast of the Sphinx nearly 1,000 years after its construction and dedicated it to the cult of Hor-em-akhet.

In Graeco-Roman times, Giza had become a tourist destination and some Roman emperors visited the Sphinx for political reasons. The Sphinx was cleared of sand in the first century AD in honor of Emperor Nero. Pliny the Elder describes the face of the Sphinx as having been colored red. The last emperor connected with the monument is Septimius Severus, around 200 AD, after which the Sphinx was once more engulfed by the sands.

Arab authors of the Middle Ages describe the Sphinx as a talisman guarding the area from the desert. Al-Maqrizi describes it as the talisman of the Nile, upon which locals believed the flood cycle depended.

In 1817, the first modern archaeological dig, supervised by Giovanni Battista Caviglia, uncovered the Sphinx's chest completely. Early Egyptologists held conflicting opinions regarding the age of the Sphinx. Gaston Maspero, second director of the Egyptian Museum in Cairo, concluded the Sphinx must predate Khafre, believing it to be the most ancient monument in Egypt. Rainer Stadelmann later concluded the style of the nemes headdress is more indicative of pharaoh Khufu, Khafre's father. In 2004, Vassil Dobrev announced evidence suggesting the Sphinx may have been the work of the little-known pharaoh Djedefre, Khafre's half-brother, who may have built it in the image of his father Khufu to identify him with the sun god Ra.

In 1931, engineers repaired the head of the Sphinx after part of its headdress had fallen off in 1926 due to erosion. Further renovations to the stone base and rock body were undertaken in the 1980s and redone in the 1990s. The middle body has disintegrated most severely because the limestone from which it is formed is softer than the surrounding rock, whereas the head was sculpted from a harder limestone more resistant to erosion.

The destruction of the Sphinx's missing nose has long been the subject of myths. The most popular — though false — story attributes it to cannonballs fired by Napoleon's army. However, drawings made by Frederic Louis Norden in 1737, over sixty years before Napoleon's arrival, already show the Sphinx without its nose. Examination of the face shows marks made by rods or chisels, suggesting the nose was deliberately pried off. Arab historian al-Maqrizi attributed the act to a Sufi Muslim named Muhammad Saim al-Dahr, who defaced the Sphinx in 1378 in an act of iconoclasm. The Sphinx may also have had a ceremonial pharaonic beard, possibly added after the original construction. Residue of red pigment is visible on the face, and traces of yellow and blue pigment have been found elsewhere, leading Egyptologist Mark Lehner to suggest the monument was once painted in vivid colours. Fragments of the beard are today preserved in the British Museum and the Egyptian Museum in Cairo.

. In some lesser accounts, there was a second riddle: "There are two sisters: one gives birth to the other and she, in turn, gives birth to the first. Who are the two sisters?" The answer is "day and night" (both words—ἡμέρα and νύξ, respectively—are feminine in Ancient Greek). This second riddle is also found in a Gascon version of the myth and could be very ancient. Bested at last, the Sphinx then threw herself from her high rock and died; or, in some versions Oedipus killed her. An alternative version tells that she devoured herself. In both cases, Oedipus can therefore be recognized as a "liminal" or threshold figure, helping effect the transition between the old religious practices, represented by the death of the Sphinx, and the rise of the new, Olympian gods.

The riddle in popular culture In Jean Cocteau's retelling of the Oedipus legend, The Infernal Machine, the Sphinx tells Oedipus the answer to the riddle in order to kill herself so that she did not have to kill any more, and also to make him love her. He leaves without ever thanking her for giving him the answer to the riddle. The scene ends when the Sphinx and Anubis ascend back to the heavens. There are mythic, anthropological, psychoanalytic and parodic interpretations of the Riddle of the Sphinx, and of Oedipus's answer to it. Sigmund Freud describes "the question of where babies come from" as a riddle of the Sphinx.

Numerous riddle books use the Sphinx in their title or illustrations.

Romania Sfinxul is a natural rock formation in the Bucegi Natural Park which is in the Bucegi Mountains of Romania. This rock formation is named for its resemblance to the Sphinx of Giza, and is located at an altitude of 2,216 metres (7,270 feet) within the Babele complex of rock formations.

Asia A composite mythological being with the body of a lion and the head of a human is present in the traditions, mythology and art of South and Southeast Asia. Variously known as puruṣamr ga (Sanskrit, "human-animal"), purushamirugam (Tamil, "human-animal"), naravirala (Sanskrit, "human-cat") in India, or as nara-simha (Sanskrit, "human-lion") in Sri Lanka, manussiha or manutthiha (Pali, "human-lion") in Myanmar, and norasingha (from Pali, "human-lion", a variation of the Sanskrit "nara-simha") or thep norasingha ("man-lion deity"), or nora nair in Thailand. Although, just like the "nara-simha", she/he has a head of a lion and the body of a human. In contrast to the sphinxes in Egypt, Mesopotamia, and Greece, of which the traditions largely have been lost due to the discontinuity of the civilization, the traditions related to the "Asian sphinxes" are very much alive today. The earliest artistic depictions of "sphinxes" from the South Asian subcontinent are to some extent influenced by Hellenistic art and writings. These hail from the period when Buddhist art underwent a phase of Hellenistic influence. Numerous sphinxes can be seen on the gateways of Bharhut stupa, dating to the 1st century B.C. In South India, the "sphinx" is known as puruṣamr ga (Sanskrit) or purushamirugam (Tamil), meaning "human-animal". It is found depicted in sculptural art in temples and palaces where it serves an apotropaic purpose, just as the "sphinxes" in other parts of the ancient world. It is said by the tradition, to take away the sins of the devotees when they enter a temple and to ward off evil in general. It is therefore often found in a strategic position on the gopuram or temple gateway, or near the entrance of the sanctum sanctorum.

The puruṣamr ga plays a significant role in daily as well as yearly ritual of South Indian Hindu temples. In the Shodhasha-Upakaara (or sixteen honors) ritual, performed between one and six times at significant sacred moments through the day, it decorates one of the lamps of the Deepaaradhana or lamp ceremony. And in several temples the puruṣamr ga is also one of the vahana or vehicles of the deity during the processions of the Brahmotsava or festival. In Kanyakumari district, in the southernmost tip of the Indian subcontinent, during the night of Maha Shivaratri, devotees run 75 kilometres while visiting and worshiping at twelve Shiva temples. This Shiva Ottam or Running for Shiva is performed in commemoration of the story of the race between the Purushamirugam and Bhima, one of the Pandavas of the Hindu Epic Mahabharata. The Indian conception of a sphinx that comes closest to the classic Greco-Roman idea is the Sharabha and Gandabherunda, two mythical creatures, part lion, part human, part mammal and part bird, and the form of Sharabha that god Shiva took on and fought with the god Vishnu as Narasimha and Shiva as Sharabha was killed by Vishnu as Gandabherunda in the form of Narashima when Narashima killed Hiranyakashipu. In Sri Lanka and India, the sphinx is known as narasimha or human-lion. As a sphinx, it has the body of a lion and the head of a human being, and is not to be confused with Narasimha, the fourth incarnation of the deity Vishnu; this avatara or incarnation of Vishnu has a human body and the head of a lion and Vishnu as Narashima killed Hiranyakashipu. The "sphinx" narasimha is part of the Buddhist tradition and functions as a guardian of the northern direction and also was depicted on banners.

In Burma (Myanmar), the sphinx-like statue, with a human head and two lion hindquarters, is known as Manussiha (manuthiha). It is depicted on the corners of Buddhist stupas, and its legends tell how it was created by Buddhist monks to protect a new-born royal baby from being devoured by ogresses. Nora Nair, Norasingha and Thep Norasingha are three of the names under which the "sphinx" is known in Thailand. They are depicted as upright walking beings with the lower body of a lion or deer, and the upper body of a human. Often they are found as female-male pairs. Here, too, the sphinx serves a protective function. It also is enumerated among the mythological creatures that inhabit the ranges of the sacred mountain Himapan.

Japanese literature Giorgio Amitrano's Echoes of Ancient Greek Myths in Murakami Haruki's novels and in Other Works of Contemporary Japanese Literature explores how Haruki Murakami's Kafka on the Shore shares thematic elements of decadence with Oedipus' myth and parallels the protagonist's journey to self-discovery. The Sphinx motif present within the novel is established through the enigmatic creature of Murakami's design, Oshima: a mysterious, omnipotent being who has the protagonist grapple with the concept of a meaningless existence in turn of searching for authenticity by disconnecting from societal conventions of wealth and status. He tests the durability of character of the novel's protagonists through a series of tests that may challenge their perception of truth regarding their existence.

Burmese Literature Lowell Edmunds' Oedipus in Burma is an explorative look on the Oedipus' myth in Burmese literature and culture. The folktale Pauk and the Dragon uses similar motifs from the Greek myth to explore Pauk's, the protagonist, road to destiny and fulfilling the quests needed to defeat the dragon: the Sphinx motif. Using intelligence, courage, and determination, Pauk defeats the dragon but not before facing the consequences of the knowledge he acquired on his journey. Decadent themes of fate, destiny, tragedy, mystery, and identity present themselves in the Burmese adaptations of Greek myths, in this case, it is Oedipus and the Sphinx.

Freemasonry The sphinx imagery has historically been adopted into Masonic architecture, symbolism and some of its rituals. Among the Egyptians, sphinxes were placed at the entrance of the temples to guard their mysteries, by warning those who penetrated within that they should conceal a knowledge of them from the uninitiated. Champollion said that the sphinx became successively the symbol of each of the gods. The placement of the sphinxes expressed the idea that all the gods were hidden from the people, and that the knowledge of them, guarded in the sanctuaries, was revealed to initiates only. As a Masonic emblem, the sphinx has been adopted as a symbol of mystery, and as such often is found as a decoration sculptured in front of Masonic temples, or engraved at the head of Masonic documents.

Similar hybrid creatures With feline features Gopaitioshah – The Persian Gopat or Gopaitioshah is another creature that is similar to the Sphinx, being a winged bull or lion with human face. The Gopat have been represented in ancient art of Iran since late second millennium BC, and was a common symbol for dominant royal power in ancient Iran. Gopats were common motifs in the art of Elamite period, Luristan, North and North West region of Iran in Iron Age, and Achaemenid art, and can be found in texts such as the Bundahishn, the Dadestan-i Denig, the Menog-i Khrad, as well as in collections of tales, such as the Matikan-e yusht faryan and in its Islamic replication, the Marzubannama. Löwenmensch figurine – The 32,000-year-old Aurignacian Löwenmensch figurine, also known as "lion-human" is the oldest known anthropomorphic statue, discovered in the Hohlenstein-Stadel, a German cave in 1939. Manticore – The Manticore (Early Middle Persian: Mardyakhor or Martikhwar, "man-eater") is a Persian legendary hybrid creature and another similar creature to the sphinx. Narasimha – Narasimha ("human-lion") is an incarnation (Avatara) of Vishnu in Hinduism in the Dashavatara of Vishnu who takes the form of half-man/half-Asiatic lion, having a human torso and lower body, but with a lion-like face and claws and in this avatara, Vishnu killed Hiranyakashipu as Narashima and saved the world from chaos in Hindu Mythology. Urmahlullu – Lion-centaurs, represented as sphinxes with arms, are attested in ancient Assyria as Urmahlullu, having a lion body below the waist and a human body above the waist.

Without feline features In ancient Assyria, bas-reliefs of shedu bulls with the crowned bearded heads of kings guarded the entrances of temples. Many Greek mythological creatures who are archaic survivals of previous mythologies with respect to the classical Olympian mythology, like the centaurs, are similar to the Sphinx.

St. Catherines Monastery.location: Saint Catherine , South Sinai Governorate , Egypt
St. Catherines Monastery.detailed_knowledge: Saint Catherine's Monastery  officially the Sacred Autonomous Royal Monastery of Saint Catherine of the Holy and God-Trodden Mount Sinai, is a Christian monastery located in the Sinai Peninsula of Egypt at the foot of Mount Sinai. It was built between 548 and 565 by order of the Byzantine emperor Justinian I and is regarded as the world's oldest continuously inhabited Christian monastery. It was constructed around the Chapel of the Burning Bush, traditionally associated with the site where Moses encountered the burning bush, and later became associated with the relics of Saint Catherine of Alexandria, which were transferred there and turned the monastery into an important pilgrimage center, eventually leading to its present name. The monastery is administered by the autonomous Church of Sinai within the Greek Orthodox tradition and was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2002 due to its outstanding religious and historical significance to Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. The complex includes a preserved Fatimid mosque dating to around 1106. It also contains one of the world's most significant libraries of early manuscripts, including the Codex Sinaiticus and Syriac Sinaiticus, as well as one of the oldest known depictions of Christ Pantocrator. The monastery lies near several sacred mountains traditionally associated with the biblical Sinai region, including Mount Sinai (Jebel Musa), Willow Peak, and Jebel Arrenziyeb. Tradition holds that the site also includes the Well of Moses, where Moses is said to have met Zipporah. The monastery is considered sacred in the three Abrahamic religions due to its biblical associations and long-standing religious traditions. Historical accounts of monastic life in the region date back to the 4th century, notably in the writings of the pilgrim Egeria. The monastery was later fortified under Justinian I, incorporating earlier chapels attributed to Empress Helena. Its architecture includes one of the oldest surviving king post roof trusses in the world. During the medieval period, the monastery became an important pilgrimage destination, especially during the Crusader era, and maintained wide regional connections across Egypt, the Levant, and the Mediterranean. In the Fatimid and Mamluk periods, it included a functioning mosque and experienced periods of both prosperity and decline, with increasing pressure from Bedouin groups and political instability leading to reduced monastic numbers. The monastery remained active through the Ottoman period and underwent restoration efforts in later centuries. In modern times, it has faced both security threats and legal disputes concerning its land ownership and administrative status, particularly in the 21st century, alongside large-scale regional development projects. The monastery's library, established between 527 and 565, is recognized as the world's oldest continuously operating library and holds one of the largest collections of early codices and manuscripts in the world, in multiple ancient languages including Greek, Syriac, Georgian, Arabic, Latin, Armenian, and others. The Codex Sinaiticus, discovered in the 19th century by Constantin von Tischendorf, is among its most significant manuscripts and is now primarily housed in the British Library after a complex history of removal and acquisition. The monastery also preserves the Syriac Sinaiticus, an important early biblical text discovered in the 1890s, and continues to be a major center for manuscript studies, including ongoing digitization and imaging projects that have revealed previously unknown texts through multispectral analysis. The site is also home to an extensive collection of early Christian icons, including some of the oldest surviving encaustic icons from the 5th and 6th centuries, as well as a large number of Crusader-style works produced in the medieval period. These artistic and manuscript collections, along with its continuous religious function and architectural preservation, make Saint Catherine's Monastery one of the most significant surviving centers of early Christian heritage in the world.

Statue of King Zoser.detailed_knowledge: Djoser, also known as Zoser and by his Hellenized names Tosorthros and Sesorthos, was an ancient Egyptian pharaoh of the Third Dynasty during the Old Kingdom and is widely regarded as the founder of that era. He was the son of King Khasekhemwy and Queen Nimaathap, although the exact nature of his succession remains debated among scholars. In contemporary inscriptions he is referred to by his Horus name Netjerikhet, meaning "divine of body," while later sources such as the Ptolemaic Famine Stela confirm the identification of Netjerikhet with Djoser. Although some king lists, including those of Manetho and the Turin Canon, name other rulers as the first kings of the Third Dynasty, many modern Egyptologists argue that Djoser was in fact its true founder, supported by seal impressions discovered at Abydos that associate him directly with Khasekhemwy's burial complex. Djoser is especially renowned for commissioning the Step Pyramid at Saqqara, the earliest monumental stone construction in ancient Egypt, which marked a major architectural innovation in funerary design and laid the foundation for later pyramid building traditions. His reign is generally considered to have lasted either nineteen or twenty-nine years, with longer estimates favored due to the scale and complexity of his construction projects and military expeditions into the Sinai Peninsula, where he oversaw the extraction of turquoise and copper and subdued local populations. His most important architectural achievement, the Step Pyramid, evolved from a traditional mastaba into a six-tiered structure reaching approximately 62 meters in height, enclosed within a vast mortuary complex surrounded by a 10.5-meter-high stone wall, making it the first large-scale structure in history built entirely from carved stone. Beneath the pyramid lies a complex network of tunnels and chambers, including a burial shaft approximately 28 meters deep sealed by a massive stone plug, and galleries containing over 40,000 stone vessels. Central to the funerary complex is the serdab chamber, which housed a painted limestone statue of Djoser, now preserved in the Egyptian Museum in Cairo, with a plaster replica still located at Saqqara in its original position. This statue, one of the oldest known life-sized Egyptian sculptures, depicts the king seated with his right arm across his chest and his left resting on his thigh, with his feet placed upon the symbol of Egypt's traditional enemies, the Nine Bows, emphasizing royal dominance and divine authority. Djoser was closely associated with his architect and vizier Imhotep, who played a central role in designing the Step Pyramid and was later deified in Greco-Roman tradition as a god of medicine. Later historical traditions, including medieval Arabic historians such as Al-Nuwayri, remembered Djoser as a wise and exemplary ruler, and his legacy persisted in Egyptian cultural memory for more than two millennia, as reflected in literary traditions and religious commemorations that continued to reference his reign and achievements.

Statue of Tutankhamun with Ankhesenamun.location: Valley of the Kings, West Bank of Luxor, Egypt
Statue of Tutankhamun with Ankhesenamun.detailed_knowledge: Statue of Tutankhamun with Ankhesenamun This statue depicts the young pharaoh Tutankhamun alongside his Great Royal Wife, Ankhesenamun. The artwork reflects the intimate relationship between the royal couple and provides valuable insight into the artistic traditions of Egypt's Eighteenth Dynasty during the late New Kingdom period. Tutankhamun ruled Egypt between approximately 1332 and 1323 BCE and is renowned for the discovery of his nearly intact tomb in the Valley of the Kings. Ankhesenamun, the daughter of Akhenaten and Nefertiti, played an important role during a period of religious and political transition in ancient Egypt. The statue emphasizes themes of royal authority, marital unity, and divine legitimacy. The figures are often portrayed in a graceful and naturalistic style characteristic of the post-Amarna artistic tradition, which retained some of the realism introduced during Akhenaten's reign while restoring more traditional Egyptian conventions. Beyond its artistic value, the sculpture serves as historical evidence of the close partnership between Tutankhamun and Ankhesenamun. Similar representations of the couple found among the treasures of Tutankhamun's tomb show scenes of affection and support, highlighting the importance of family and kingship in ancient Egyptian culture. Today, statues and representations of Tutankhamun and Ankhesenamun remain among the most recognizable symbols of ancient Egypt, offering visitors a glimpse into the lives of one of history's most famous royal couples.

Temple of Habu.detailed_knowledge: Medinet Habu is an archaeological locality situated near the foot of the Theban Hills on the West Bank of the River Nile opposite the modern city of Luxor, Egypt, and is today associated almost synonymously with the Mortuary Temple of Ramesses III, the largest and best preserved structure at the site. The mortuary temple is best known as the source of inscribed reliefs depicting the advent and defeat of the Sea Peoples during the reign of Ramesses III, including the Battle of the Delta, and some of its building materials were reused from earlier monuments including the destroyed mortuary temple of Tausret, the last pharaoh of the Nineteenth Dynasty. The Greco-Roman period temple to Isis known as Deir el-Shelwit lies 4 kilometres to the south, and reused inscribed blocks from Medinet Habu were found there. The site hosted a continuous human settlement from pharaonic times until the 9th century, by which point it had become a Coptic centre called Jeme, the last remnants of which were cleared during excavations at the end of the 19th century. Adjacent to Medinet Habu, north of the outer wall, lies the poorly preserved memorial temple of Ay and Horemheb; originally built by Ay around 1323 to 1319 BC, it was later usurped by Horemheb, who removed all inscriptions and images of Ay. A large quartzite statue excavated from the ruins of this temple in the 1930s, now on display at the Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures, bears traces of earlier cartouches confirming it was originally a statue of Tutankhamun, subsequently overwritten by Ay and then Horemheb. The Oriental Institute of Chicago also excavated a large late Roman period cemetery to the northwest of Medinet Habu, recovering 66 mummy tags inscribed in Greek. The origins of the name Medinet Habu are unknown; the earliest European attestations date to cartographers of the 17th and 18th centuries, and proposed etymologies deriving it from the Coptic name for Luxor or from the name of the deified official Amenhotep son of Hapu are not considered plausible, as neither explains the final long vowel. Folk etymology attributes the name to a mythical king named Habu, while the old Arabic name of the place, Gabal Shama, derives from the Coptic Djami, which in turn comes from the Ancient Egyptian word of unclear etymology. In Greek antiquity the area was known as Memnonia or Kastron Memnonionos, a name that survives in the designation Colossi of Memnon. The first European to describe the temple in modern literature was Vivant Denon, who visited between 1799 and 1801. Jean-François Champollion spent a fortnight and a half at the site in 1829 as part of the Franco-Tuscan Expedition, and a Prussian expedition led by Karl Richard Lepsius worked there from November 1844 until April 1845. Sporadic clearance work took place between 1859 and 1899 under the Egyptian Department of Antiquities, during which a substantial Byzantine church in the second court and numerous Greco-Roman period buildings were destroyed without records being taken. Theodore M. Davis excavated the Palace of Ramesses III in 1913, and since 1924 the Architectural and Epigraphic Surveys of the University of Chicago Oriental Institute have conducted almost continuous recording and conservation work at the site, with the Epigraphic Survey continuing to the present day under a succession of directors including Harold Hayden Nelson, Richard Anthony Parker, George Hughes, Charles Nims, Edward Wente, Kent Weeks, Lanny Bell, Peter Dorman, and W. Raymond Johnson. The Mortuary Temple of Ramesses III is approximately 150 metres long, of orthodox design, and closely resembles the nearby Ramesseum of Ramesses II. Its full formal name was the Temple of Usermare-Meriamon United-with-Eternity in the estate of Amon on the West of Thebes. The temple precinct measures approximately 210 by 300 metres and contains more than 7,000 square metres of decorated wall reliefs across 48 rooms, of which 8 constitute the Mortuary Suite devoted to the funerary process of Ramesses III as Osiride king. The entire south wall is inscribed with a liturgical calendar of 1,470 lines of hieroglyphs listing offerings for daily, monthly, and annual feasts, derived largely from the Ramesseum text. The first pylon leads into an open courtyard lined with colossal statues of Ramesses III as Osiris, the second pylon into a peristyle hall, and the third pylon through a columned portico into a large hypostyle hall. Reliefs and actual heads of foreign captives were placed within the temple to symbolise royal dominance over Syria and Nubia, and some temple carvings were later altered during the Coptic period. Ten tombs dating from the 6th to 8th century BC were found in the inner sanctum, including that of Nesterwy, daughter of the 23rd Dynasty pharaoh Rudamun. The temple carries four major battle inscriptions: the Year 5 inscription describing the First Libyan War; the Year 8 inscription detailing the Northern War against the Sea Peoples, whose confederation comprised the Peleset, Theker, Shekelesh, Denyen, and Weshesh; the Year 11 inscription recording the Second Libyan or Meshwesh War, in which Ramesses III crushed a western Libyan tribe that had attempted to occupy the Egyptian Delta; and the Year 12 inscription summarising the king's deeds to that point. The western and northern walls carry military reliefs depicting conflicts with Nubians and Libyans, showing battle preparations, divine intervention, the distribution of weapons, lion hunts, and the presentation of booty to the gods, including a celebrated scene in which Ramesses III and his troops rain arrows down on opponents in ships. The front pylon bears two topographical lists of place names in oval name-shields, with 125 mostly Levantine entries on the south tower and mostly African and Nubian localities on the northern tower, both surmounted by scenes of Ramesses III smiting enemies. The temple also contains a minor king list known as the Medinet Habu king list, showing Ramesses III participating in the Festival of Min with a procession of 16 cartouches naming nine ancestral pharaohs, first fully published by the Epigraphic Survey in 1940. The temple portico displays processions of royal sons on both sides of the west wall doorway, with names and titles added at a later date for ten of the figures, and a shorter procession of unidentified royal daughters on the south wall. The mortuary temple grounds are enclosed by a mudbrick temenos wall measuring 315 by 210 metres, rising 18 metres high and 10 metres thick, enclosing 66,000 square metres with fortified gates in the eastern and western walls. The outer court originally included a large garden with a sacred pool measuring 20 by 18 metres, described in Papyrus Harris I. The mudbrick Palace of Ramesses III occupied the area south of the temple between the pylons, used by the king on religious festival occasions and intended as his Mansion of Millions of Years after death; it had two construction phases, the second of which added a full second storey and expanded the throne complex substantially. The Small Temple, located just to the right of the mortuary temple entrance, has remains dating to at least the early New Kingdom and possibly the Middle Kingdom, and was originally dedicated to the Ogdoad of eight gods associated with Amun. Hatshepsut undertook restoration work there but died before completing it, and Thutmose III finished the project while excising all mentions of Hatshepsut; the temple subsequently underwent alterations in the 20th, 25th, 26th, 29th, and 30th dynasties and the Greco-Roman period. In the northeast corner of the outer court, four combined mausoleums and mortuary shrines known as the Saite Chapels were built between the 8th and 6th centuries BC for the God's Wife of Amun, housing the chapels of Shepenwepet I, Amenardis I, Shepenwepet II, and Ankhnesneferibré, and constructed mainly of Nubian siliceous sandstone from Gebel el-Silsila. The tomb of Harsiese, a Third Intermediate Period ruler of Thebes dating to around 880 to 860 BC, was discovered in 1932, its substructure intact with an inclined entrance passage, antechamber, and burial chamber containing a reused granite coffin made for Henutmire, sister of Ramesses II. The remains of the residence of Butehamon, a royal scribe and overseer of the royal treasury in the Theban Necropolis during the 20th and 21st dynasties around 1070 BC, were found in the outer court near the Western Fortified Gate. The Coptic settlement at Medinet Habu developed as the final stage of continuous occupation of the complex from pharaonic through Roman and late antique times, ultimately housing an estimated population of 18,860 residents in densely packed urban blocks with narrow streets, culminating in the construction of the great five-aisled Holy Church of Djeme in the second court of the temple, dated by scholars to between the 5th and 7th centuries, before the settlement was abandoned in the 9th century.

Temple of Hathor.detailed_knowledge: Hathor was a major goddess in ancient Egyptian religion who played a wide variety of roles, serving as a sky deity, solar goddess, goddess of music, dance, joy, love, sexuality, and maternal care, and as a guide of deceased souls into the afterlife. As a sky deity she was the mother or consort of the sky god Horus and the sun god Ra, both connected with kingship, making her the symbolic mother of the pharaohs. She was one of several goddesses who acted as the Eye of Ra, his feminine counterpart, and in this form possessed a vengeful aspect that protected him from his enemies. More temples were dedicated to her than to any other Egyptian goddess, and her most prominent temple was at Dendera in Upper Egypt. Images of cattle appear frequently in Predynastic Egyptian art alongside images of women with upraised curved arms reminiscent of bovine horns, and the Gerzeh Palette from the Naqada II period depicts a cow's head surrounded by stars suggesting a connection with the sky. Hathor is not unambiguously mentioned or depicted until the Fourth Dynasty of the Old Kingdom, when she rose rapidly to prominence, supplanting an early crocodile god at Dendera to become its patron deity and increasingly absorbing the cult of the goddess Bat, with whom she fused in the Middle Kingdom. She ascended alongside Ra and became his mythological wife, thereby becoming the divine mother of the pharaoh. Egyptian texts often speak of her manifestations as the Seven Hathors or even as many as 362 distinct forms, leading the Egyptologist Robyn Gillam to describe her as a type of deity rather than a single entity. As a sky goddess she was given the epithets mistress of the sky and mistress of the stars, and was commonly called the Golden One in reference to the sun's radiance. She was a member of the divine entourage accompanying Ra in his solar barque and was sometimes fused with the goddess Nebethetepet. She served as one of many goddesses to take the role of the Eye of Ra, a feminine personification of the sun disk and an extension of Ra's power, and her roles as mother, wife, and daughter of Ra reflected the daily cycle of the sun. A group of myths known from the New Kingdom onward describe the Eye goddess rampaging uncontrolled; in the Book of the Heavenly Cow, Ra sends Hathor as the Eye of Ra to punish rebellious humans, whereupon she becomes the lioness Sekhmet and massacres them until Ra causes her to drink red-dyed beer, mistaking it for blood, after which she reverts to her benign form. Related to this is the myth of the Distant Goddess, in which the Eye goddess rebels and rampages in Libya or Nubia until a god such as Thoth is sent to pacify and return her. Hathor's epithets link her to celebration, music, dance, garlands, myrrh, and drunkenness, and the sistrum, a rattle-like instrument with erotic connotations, was particularly important in her worship. Her sexual and procreative aspects were fundamental to her character; she could serve as the Hand of Atum in creation myths, and she was called mistress of love and praised in love poetry from the Twentieth Dynasty. She was considered the mother of various child deities and was regarded as both Horus's mother and consort, making her the divine counterpart of human queens. The first images of the Hathor-cow suckling the king date to the reign of Mentuhotep II, and queens were increasingly portrayed as embodying the goddess from the Middle Kingdom onward, with queens bearing her headdress from the late Eighteenth Dynasty. Hathor was connected with foreign lands and trade, particularly with Nubia, Canaan, Byblos, and the Sinai Peninsula, where she was worshipped as Lady of Mefkat in connection with turquoise and other minerals, and as patroness of the Egyptian copper and turquoise mines at Serabit el-Khadim. She was frequently invoked in private prayers and votive offerings, particularly by women desiring children, and fertility and safe childbirth were among the most prominent concerns in her popular worship. Hathor crossed the boundary between the living and the dead and was closely linked with tomb sites and necropolises; the Theban necropolis was often portrayed as a stylized mountain with the cow of Hathor emerging from it, and she appeared on tombs welcoming the deceased into the afterlife. In the Third Intermediate Period Egyptians began adding Hathor's name to that of deceased women in place of Osiris, and in late periods she was sometimes said to rule the afterlife as Osiris did. Her most common iconographic form was a woman wearing a headdress of cow horns and a sun disk, often with a red or turquoise sheath dress, though she could also appear as a cow bearing the sun disk between her horns, as a woman with a cow's head, as a lioness, a cobra, a sycamore tree, or a human face with bovine ears. She carried the sistrum and menat necklace as her primary symbols, and mirrors were also associated with her because of their solar and feminine connotations. Hathoric columns featuring her face were used in many temples from the late Old Kingdom onward. During the Old Kingdom most priests of Hathor were women, many from the royal family, though women were gradually excluded from the highest priestly positions in the Middle Kingdom. Her most important cult center shifted from Memphis to Dendera after the Old Kingdom, and the last version of the Dendera temple, built in the Ptolemaic and Roman periods, is among the best-preserved Egyptian temples surviving today. Major annual festivals included the Festival of Drunkenness commemorating the return of the Eye of Ra, the Festival of the Beautiful Reunion in which Hathor's statue from Dendera traveled by boat to the Temple of Horus at Edfu where the divine couple performed rites for entombed primordial gods, and the new year ceremony in which the Hathor statue was carried to the temple roof to be bathed in sunlight. During the New Kingdom goddesses such as Mut and Isis began encroaching on Hathor's position, and after the New Kingdom Isis increasingly overshadowed her, though Hathor continued to be venerated until the extinction of ancient Egyptian religion in the early centuries AD. In the Ptolemaic period the Greeks sometimes called Hathor by the name Aphrodite, and traits of Isis, Hathor, and Aphrodite were combined in the portrayal of Ptolemaic queens as goddesses. The Nubians fully incorporated Hathor into their religion during the New Kingdom, and several pharaohs including Amenhotep III and Ramesses II built temples in Nubia that celebrated their queens as manifestations of her.

Temple of Hatshepsut.detailed_knowledge: The mortuary temple of Hatshepsut was built during the reign of Pharaoh Hatshepsut of the Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt. Located opposite the city of Luxor, it is considered to be a masterpiece of ancient architecture. Its three massive terraces rise above the desert floor and into the cliffs of Deir el-Bahari. Hatshepsut's tomb, KV20, lies inside the same massif capped by El Qurn, a pyramid for her mortuary complex. At the edge of the desert, 1 km east, connected to the complex by a causeway, lies the accompanying valley temple. Across the river Nile, the whole structure points towards the monumental Eighth Pylon, Hatshepsut's most recognizable addition to the Temple of Karnak and the site from which the procession of the Beautiful Festival of the Valley departed. Its axes identify the temple's twin functions: its central east-west axis served to receive the barque of Amun-Re at the climax of the festival, while its north-south axis represented the life cycle of the pharaoh from coronation to rebirth. The terraced temple was constructed between Hatshepsut's seventh and twentieth regnal years, during which building plans were repeatedly modified. In its design, it was heavily influenced by the adjacent Temple of Mentuhotep II of the Eleventh Dynasty built six centuries earlier. In the arrangement of its chambers and sanctuaries, though, the temple is wholly unique. The central axis, customarily reserved for the mortuary complex, is occupied instead by the sanctuary of the barque of Amun-Re, with the mortuary cult being displaced south to form the auxiliary axis with the solar cult complex to the north. Separated from the main sanctuary are shrines to Hathor and Anubis, which lie on the middle terrace. The porticoes that front the terrace here host the most notable reliefs of the temple, those of the expedition to the Land of Punt and the divine birth of Hatshepsut which form the backbone of her case to rightfully occupy the throne as a member of the royal family and as godly progeny. Two decades after Hatshepsut's death, under the direction of Thutmose III, references to her rule were erased, usurped, or obliterated. The campaign was intense but brief, quelled after two years when Amenhotep II was enthroned. In the Amarna Period, the temple was defaced again when Akhenaten ordered the images of Egyptian gods, particularly those of Amun, to be erased. These damages were repaired subsequently under Tutankhamun, Horemheb and Ramesses II. An earthquake in the Third Intermediate Period caused further harm. During the Ptolemaic period, the sanctuary of Amun was restructured, and a new portico was built at its entrance. A Coptic monastery of Saint Phoibammon was built between the 6th and 8th centuries AD, and images of Christ were painted over original reliefs. The latest graffito left is dated to around 1223. The temple resurfaces in the records of the modern era in 1737 with Richard Pococke, a British traveller, who visited the site. Several visitations followed though serious excavation was not conducted until the 1850s and 60s under Auguste Mariette. The temple was fully excavated between 1893 and 1906 during an expedition of the Egypt Exploration Fund directed by Edouard Naville. Further efforts were carried out by Herbert E. Winlock and the Metropolitan Museum of Art from 1911 to 1936, and by Emile Baraize and the Egyptian Antiquities Service from 1925 to 1952. Since 1961, the Polish Centre of Mediterranean Archaeology has carried out extensive consolidation and restoration works throughout the temple, and it was opened to the public in March 2023. Hatshepsut renewed the act of monument building following her accession to the throne, focusing her activities on the city of Thebes and the god Amun. To the Temple of Karnak she contributed the Eighth Pylon, a pair of 30.5 metre tall obelisks, offering chapels, a shrine with another obelisk pair, and statues of herself. Nearly the entire mortuary temple was built of limestone, with some red granite and sandstone. The identity of the architect behind the project remains unclear, though it is possible that Senenmut, the Overseer of Works, or Hapuseneb, the High Priest, were responsible. The opening feature of the temple is three terraces fronted by a portico and arrived at by a 1 km long causeway from the valley temple. The lower terrace measures 120 metres deep by 75 metres wide and featured two Persea trees, two T-shaped basins holding papyri and flowers, and two recumbent lion statues. The south portico's reliefs depict the transportation of two obelisks from Elephantine to Karnak, while the north portico's reliefs depict Hatshepsut as a sphinx crushing her enemies along with images of fishing, hunting, and offerings to the gods. The middle terrace measures 75 metres deep by 90 metres wide, and its west porticoes contain the most notable reliefs of the temple: the southwest portico depicts the expedition to the Land of Punt and the transportation of exotic goods to Thebes, while the northwest portico reliefs narrate the divine birth of Hatshepsut to Thutmose I, represented as Amun-Re, and Ahmose, constituting the oldest known scene of its type. In the southwest and northwest corners of the middle terrace are the shrines to Hathor and Anubis respectively. The Hathor shrine at the south end of the middle terrace is accessed by a ramp and adorned with four columns carrying Hathor capitals, containing two hypostyle halls and a double sanctuary, with reliefs depicting Hathor with Hatshepsut and the goddess Weret-hekhau presenting the pharaoh with a Menat necklace. The Anubis shrine at the north end comprises a hypostyle hall with 12 columns followed by two rooms terminating at a small niche. The upper terrace opens to 26 columns each fronted by a 5.2 metre tall Osiride statue of Hatshepsut, split in the centre by a granite gate through which the festival courtyard was entered. The courtyard is surrounded by pillars and its walls are carved with the Beautiful Festival of the Valley on the north, the Festival of Opet on the east, and coronation rituals on the south. The sanctuary of Amun lies at the back of the temple on the main axis, described by Hatshepsut as a garden for my father Amun, and served as the endpoint of the annual Beautiful Festival of the Valley. Its first chamber hosted the barque of Amun with a skylight flooding the statue with light, its second chamber contained a cult image of Amun flanked by chapels carved with the Heliopolitan and Theban Enneads, and its third chamber contained a statue for the Daily Ritual. The solar cult court is accessed from the upper terrace courtyard and hosts a grand altar open to the sky. The mortuary cult complex in the south of the courtyard contains two offering halls, the northern dedicated to Thutmose I and the southern to Hatshepsut, the latter being the largest chamber in the entire temple at 13.25 metres deep by 5.25 metres wide with a vaulted ceiling 6.35 metres high, directly emulating the offering halls of Old and Middle Kingdom pyramid complexes and copying scenes from the Pyramid of Pepi II. The Beautiful Festival of the Valley was conducted annually beginning at the Temple of Karnak, with a procession led by Hatshepsut and Thutmose III carrying Amun's barque across the Nile, along the 1 km causeway flanked by more than 100 sandstone sphinxes, through the entrance gate and up the temple's great ramps into the sanctuary where the barque and Amun were kept for a night. The daily ritual involved priests collecting water before dawn, preparing offerings, purifying the naos of Amun-Re with incense, opening the shrine, removing, cleaning, repainting, anointing, and re-dressing the cult image, presenting offerings, and closing the shrine before purifying libations were poured at noon and in the evening. Two decades after Hatshepsut's death in Thutmose III's forty-second regnal year, he ordered all evidence of her reign as king erased, employing methods ranging from scratching out feminine pronouns to chiselling away her image and titles, replacing her image with offering tables or members of the Thutmosid family, and obliterating statuary by dragging it to two designated sites and breaking it apart with sledgehammers. The proscription was abandoned two years later when Amenhotep II ascended the throne. During the Amarna Period Akhenaten ordered the erasure of images of the gods particularly Amun, with restorations made under Tutankhamun, Horemheb, and Ramesses II. An earthquake in the ninth century BC caused further damage during the Third Intermediate Period, when the temple was also used as a burial ground for priests and royalty, and a cache of forty royal mummies was moved to a hidden tomb nearby to prevent desecration. Under Ptolemy III a stone chapel was built for Asklepios, and under Ptolemy VIII the sanctuary of Amun was significantly altered to incorporate chapels for Amenhotep son of Hapu, Imhotep, and Hygieia. Between the 6th and 8th centuries AD a Coptic monastery of Saint Phoibammon was constructed on the temple grounds with figures of Christ painted over original reliefs.

Temple of Horus at Edfu.detailed_knowledge: The Temple of Edfu is an Egyptian temple located on the west bank of the Nile in Edfu, Upper Egypt. The city was known in the Hellenistic period in Koine Greek as Apollonopolis Magna, after the chief god Horus, who was identified as Apollo under the interpretatio graeca. It is one of the best preserved shrines in Egypt. The temple was built in the Ptolemaic Kingdom between 237 and 57 BC. The inscriptions on its walls provide important information on language, myth and religion during the Hellenistic period in Egypt. In particular, the temple's inscribed building texts provide details of its construction, and also preserve information about the mythical interpretation of this and all other temples as the Island of Creation. There are also important scenes and inscriptions of the Sacred Drama which related the age-old conflict between Horus and Seth. Edfu was one of several temples built during the Ptolemaic Kingdom, including the Dendera Temple complex, Esna, the Temple of Kom Ombo, and Philae. Its size reflects the relative prosperity of the time. The present temple, which was begun on 23 August 237 BC, initially consisted of a pillared hall, two transverse halls, and a barque sanctuary surrounded by chapels. The building was started during the reign of Ptolemy III Euergetes and completed in 57 BC under Ptolemy XII Auletes. It was built on the site of an earlier, smaller temple also dedicated to Horus, although the previous structure was oriented east-west rather than north-south as in the present site. A ruined pylon lies just to the east of the current temple; inscriptional evidence has been found indicating a building program under the New Kingdom rulers Ramesses I, Seti I and Ramesses II. A naos of Nectanebo II, a relic from an earlier building, is preserved in the inner sanctuary, which stands alone while the temple's barque sanctuary is surrounded by nine chapels. Over the centuries, the temple became buried to a depth of 12 metres beneath drifting desert sand and layers of river silt deposited by the Nile. Local inhabitants built homes directly over the former temple grounds. Only the upper reaches of the temple pylons were visible by 1798, when the temple was identified by a French expedition. In 1860 Auguste Mariette, a French Egyptologist, began the work of freeing Edfu temple from the sands. The Temple of Edfu is nearly intact and a good example of an ancient Egyptian temple. Its archaeological significance and high state of preservation have made it a center for tourism in Egypt and a frequent stop for the many riverboats that cruise the Nile. In 2005, access to the temple was revamped with the addition of a visitor center and paved carpark. A lighting system was added in late 2006 to allow night visits. The temple of Edfu is the largest temple dedicated to Horus and Hathor of Dendera. It was the center of several festivals sacred to Horus. Each year, Hathor travelled south from her temple at Dendera to visit Horus at Edfu, and this event marking their sacred marriage was the occasion of a great festival and pilgrimage. The creation myth of the temple of Edfu consists of several related scenes, which are found primarily on the inside of the perimeter walls of the temple. They tell the story of the beginning of the world, when it was still entirely covered by water. During the struggle between land and primeval water, the land managed to come close to the surface. Where this happened, reeds grew with the help of a falcon, which were strengthened by the gods The Far and The Large. The reeds were the germ cell for the temple of Edfu, and here Horus landed, as a falcon. A force approached, in the form of a bird, and fed Horus, the lord of Edfu; this ritual was the beginning of the cult of Edfu. The snakelike Apophis tried to impede the creation. Horus shuddered in fear, yet a harpoon, one of the forms of Ptah, came to the rescue. The enemy was defeated and the creation continued. A falcon formed the sky dome, its wings reaching from horizon to horizon, and the sun began its daily cycle. Then the first temple of Edfu was designed by the gods Thoth and Seshat, one responsible for wisdom, the other for scripture. The godly master-builder constructed the temple according to these plans, but initially not of stone, but of reed. The foundation ritual of the temple consists of multiple elements: first, the ground-plan was laid out with the stretching-the-cord ritual. When the construction was completed, the king handed the temple over to a triad of gods. To protect the building against external threats, 60 gods formed a living wall around the temple. The Temple of Edfu provides the model for the Temple Works in Holbeck, Leeds. The courtyard columns at Edfu are closely copied in the frontage of the Works.

Temple of Seti I at Abydos.class_name: Temple of Seti I at Abydos
Temple of Seti I at Abydos.detailed_knowledge: The Temple of Seti I, also known as the Great Temple of Abydos, known in antiquity as Menmaatre Happy in Abydos, is a significant historical site in Abydos, Egypt. Abydos is a significant location with its connection to kingship due to being the burial site of the proto-kings from the Pre-Dynastic period, First Dynasty kings, and the location of the Cult of Osiris. Initially, construction started in the 13th century BC by the 19th Dynasty Pharaoh Seti I, also known as Sethos, but was not completed by the time of his death; instead, construction was renovated by his son Ramesses II. The temple was built in order to commemorate and worship the earlier pharaohs of Egypt as well as the major gods of the Egyptian pantheon. Ramesses renovated the temple by changing its original design and placing inscriptions within the temple. The Temple of Seti I was designed with a standard layout from the Ramesside period featuring an L-shaped design constructed of limestone and sandstone possibly brought from Gebel Silsila. The temple boasts many features, including the first and second courts that house hypostyle halls, chapels to Seti I and various gods, and the Osireion. The South wing houses the Gallery of the Kings, which leads to a slaughter court and four secondary rooms, which continues into the Corridor of Bulls with a stairway that exits into storage rooms, and the Hall of Barques. The temple is also notable for the Abydos graffiti which is ancient Phoenician and Aramaic graffiti found on the temple walls. The temple additionally contains graffito from the 21st dynasty till the Roman period, ranging from Aramaic, Phoenician, Carian, Greek and Cypriot. In 1863 the temple was cleared and reconstructed by Auguste Mariette. In 1901-1902, Flinders Petrie, his wife Hilda Petrie, and Margaret A. Murray further excavated the temple and discovered the Osireion. After World War I, the temple was studied by Henri Edouard Naville and then by Henri Frankfort. During the 1925-1927 season under the Egyptian Exploration Society and Oriental Institute of the University of Chicago, Herbert Felton recorded the sculptures within the temple through photography. Amice M. Calverley then detailed the temple walls under Aylward M. Blackman, with financial assistance from John D. Rockefeller Jr., completing a four-volume work titled The Temple of King Sethos I At Abydos in 1933. John Baines worked the site in 1979 and again from 1981-1983. The Temple of Seti did not serve as a mortuary temple to carry the Pharaoh cult; instead, it was created to be a part of the Osiris festival, possibly being used as the final destination on a processional route connected to the Osiris Temple. The temple was reused several times as Greek, Cypriot, Phoenician, and Aramaic inscriptions make references to the Cult of Osiris. During the Late Hellenistic and Early Roman period, graffiti related the temple to the god Serapis, and its final use came from the Late Roman Period, being used as an ocular incubation for the god Bes, where individuals would leave offerings, consult the oracle, and sometimes sleep overnight in the temple to have a mystical experience. The temple contains two courts, the first entered through a pylon entrance that portrays Isis, Osiris, Ramesses, and Merenptah, containing inscriptions of Ramesses' battle scenes and scenes of Seti, while the second court contains scenes of Ramesses interacting with gods to commemorate his achievements and acceptance as ruler. The temple's two hypostyle halls contain columns and reliefs, with twelve columns and reliefs carved by Ramesses II in the first hall, and both halls divided by a wall consisting of 36 columns total. In the Gallery of Ancestors, also referred to as the Gallery of the List, one can find the Abydos King List, depicted in low relief carved under the reign of Ramesses II, showing Seti and Ramesses making offerings to their royal ancestors, with some rulers such as the 15th Dynasty Hyksos and the 18th Dynasty Akhenaten omitted from the list due to association with periods of internal weakness and divisions. The slaughterhouse provided access to the desert through its eastern entrance and was connected to the temple through the Gallery of Ancestors, consisting of a slaughter court, hall, and three rooms, each containing depictions of offerings, animal butchery, and priests carrying offerings for processions. The temple contains six barque chapels completed by Seti I's death, dedicated to the gods Osiris, Isis, Horus, Amen-Re, Re-Harakti, and Ptah, with one additional chapel belonging to Seti himself meant to represent him as a god. The south wall of the Osiris Chapel features the Osiris Fetish, which establishes a connection between solar imagery and the god Osiris, containing representations of lions possibly related to the god Aker symbolizing the horizons and sunrise and sunset, a traditional henu gesture signifying obedience and submission, and rams adorned with the solar headdress possibly representing the sun crossing into the underworld as the god Mendes. The Osireion is the symbolic tomb of Osiris, created of red granite and sandstone, housing a sarcophagus and a chest for canopic jars, possibly surrounded with floodwater to grow barley for the symbolic resurrection of Osiris, located behind the temple chapels in the shape of a rectangle measuring 57 metres by 13 metres and consisting of eight rooms, linked via transverse access from the Osiris chapel to a staircase descending into a symbolic tomb of the God Osiris where Osiris's Cenotaph was located, crafted in an 18th Dynasty tomb design mimicking those of the Valley of the Kings, with entrances adorned with religious texts such as the Book of the Dead and the Book of Caves completed by Seti's grandson Merenptah. Within ancient Egypt, major temples were considered reserve banks holding excessive amounts of grain, and the Temple of Seti I was granted special permission to mine within the Eastern Desert at Wadi Mia and within Nubia at Nauri. The Kanais Decree of Seti I protected the gold mines that supplied gold for the temple at Abydos, and the Abydos Decree by Seti I at Nauri, carved into the Cliff of Nauri, declared that the temple's property, gold mines, boats, goods, and workers belonged to the temple and were protected from seizure by magistrates. Dorothy Louise Eady, also known as Omm Sety, who lived from 16 January 1904 to 21 April 1981, was the keeper of the Temple of Seti I, believing herself to be the reincarnation of a priestess who had been the former lover of Seti, and after moving to Abydos in 1956 she practiced the ancient Egyptian religion and gave tours through the temple following her retirement. The helicopter hieroglyphs found at the temple result from carved stone being reused over time, with the initial carving made during the reign of Seti I translating as He who repulses the nine enemies of Egypt, later filled with plaster and re-carved during the reign of Ramesses II with the title He who protects Egypt and overthrows the foreign countries, with the plaster eroding over time to leave both inscriptions partially visible and creating a palimpsest-like effect of overlapping hieroglyphs.

Temple of the Oracle of Amun at Siwa.class_name: Temple of the Oracle of Amun at Siwa
Temple of the Oracle of Amun at Siwa.location: Aghurmi, Siwa Oasis, Matrouh Governorate, Egypt
Temple of the Oracle of Amun at Siwa.detailed_knowledge: The Siwa Oasis is an urban oasis in Egypt situated between the Qattara Depression and the Great Sand Sea in the Western Desert, 50 kilometres east of the Egypt-Libya border and 560 kilometres from Cairo. It is famed for its role in ancient Egypt as the home to an oracle of Amun, the ruins of which are a popular tourist attraction, giving it the ancient name Oasis of Amun-Ra. The oasis is in a deep depression that reaches 19 metres below sea level, is the site of about 200 natural springs, and supports the cultivation of thousands of date palms and olives. The Ancient Egyptian name of the oasis was meaning Field of Trees, and the etymology of the Arabic name Siwah remains unclear, with various scholars linking it to Coptic, Berber tribal names, or the Shilha Berber word asiwan meaning a type of bird of prey and hence to Amun-Ra, one of whose symbols was the falcon. Although the site is known to have been settled since at least the 10th millennium BCE, the earliest evidence of any connection with Ancient Egypt is the 26th Dynasty, when a necropolis was established. Ancient Greek settlers at Cyrene made contact with the oasis around the same time in the 7th century BCE, and the oracle temple of Amun, whom Herodotus was told took the image here of a ram, became one of the most celebrated oracles of the ancient world. During his campaign to conquer the Persian Empire, Alexander the Great reached the oasis supposedly by following birds across the desert, and the oracle confirmed him as both a divine personage and the legitimate Pharaoh of Egypt. The first European to document a visit since Roman times was the English traveller William George Browne, who came in 1792 to see the ancient temple of the Oracle of Amun. Siwa was annexed by Muhammad Ali of Egypt in 1820, and Egypt began to assert firmer control after a 1928 visit by King Fuad I. Siwa was also the site of fighting during both World War I and World War II, serving as a base for the British Army's Long Range Desert Group, though Rommel's Afrika Korps also took possession three times. The ancient fortress of Siwa, known as the Shali Ghadi, was built on natural rock and made of kershif salt and mud-brick and palm logs, and after being damaged by three days of heavy rains in 1926 it was abandoned, with only a mosque having been repaired and remaining in use. Other local historic sites include the remains of the oracle temple, the Gebel al Mawta Roman-era necropolis featuring dozens of rock-cut tombs, and Cleopatra's Bath, an antique natural spring. The traditional culture of Siwa shows many unique elements reflecting its longstanding links with isolated oasis life, as its inhabitants are Siwi Berbers belonging to 10 tribes who speak an eastern Berber language. Until a tarmac road was built to the Mediterranean coast in the 1980s, Siwa's only links with the outside world were by arduous camel tracks through the desert used to export dates and olives, bring trade goods, or carry pilgrims on the route linking the Maghreb to Cairo and hence to Mecca. As a result of this isolation, Siwis developed a unique culture manifested in crafts of basketry, pottery, silverwork and embroidery and in its distinctive style of dress, most notably the elaborate silver jewelry worn by women at weddings and ceremonies, including the huge silver disc called adrim and the round necklace called aghraw, with the full ensemble for a wealthy woman weighing as much as five or six kilos. Siwi people celebrate Ramadan, Eid al-Fitr, and Eid al-Adha, and also observe the unique Siyaha Festival in honour of the town's traditional patron saint Sidi Sulayman, during which Siwi men meet on the mountain Gabal Al-Dakrour to eat together, sing chants, and reconcile with one another over three days, ending with a march to Sidi Solayman square declaring the beginning of a new year without hatred or grudge. Siwa Oasis is about 80 kilometres in length and 20 kilometres wide, with about 25,000 people mostly Siwi Berbers, and the municipality hosts the easternmost Berber-speaking community whose language called Siwi shares many linguistic features with the languages of Sokna and El Foqaha in Libya. Date palm cultivation is by far the largest component of the oasis economy, with olive cultivation in a distant second place, and tourism has in recent decades become a vital source of income. In the mid-20th century, Egyptian archaeologist Ahmed Fakhry worked extensively at Siwa, and in 1995 Greek archaeologist Liana Souvaltzi announced that she had identified the tomb of Alexander the Great in the oasis, a claim that remains contested. An extremely old hominid footprint was discovered in 2007 at Siwa Oasis, with Egyptian scientists claiming it could be 2 to 3 million years old, which would make it the oldest fossilized hominid footprint ever found, though no proof of this conjecture was ever presented. In late 2013, an announcement was made regarding the archaeoastronomy discovery of precise spring and fall equinox sunrise alignments over the Aghurmi mound and Amun Oracle when viewed from Timasirayn temple 12 kilometres away across Lake Siwa, with the first known recent public viewing occurring on March 21, 2014. Siwa Oasis has also appeared in popular culture, featuring as an official map for Wolfenstein: Enemy Territory, as the setting of the fifth mission of Sniper Elite III, and as the birthplace of the protagonist in the videogame Assassin's Creed: Origins.

Temple_of_Isis_in_Philae.detailed_knowledge: The Philae temple complex is an island-based temple complex in the reservoir of the Aswan Low Dam, downstream of the Aswan Dam and Lake Nasser, Egypt. Originally located on Philae Island near the First Cataract of the Nile in Upper Egypt, the complex was dismantled and relocated to the nearby island of Agilkia between 1977 and 1980 to save it from flooding caused by the construction of the Aswan High Dam. The island of Philae, formerly not more than 380 metres long and 120 metres broad and composed of syenite, was known in antiquity as the Pearl of the Nile and was mentioned by numerous ancient writers including Strabo, Diodorus Siculus, Ptolemy, Seneca, and Pliny the Elder. The islands of Philae were not merely sacerdotal abodes but also centres of commerce between Meroe and Memphis, as the rapids of the cataracts were at most seasons not navigable and commodities exchanged between Egypt and Nubia were landed and re-embarked at Syene and Philae. The most ancient structure on the island was a temple for Isis built in the reign of Nectanebo I during 380 to 362 BC, and the first religious building was likely a shrine built by Pharaoh Taharqa of the 25th Dynasty, while the oldest temple to have undoubtedly stood on the island was a small kiosk built by Psamtik II of the 26th Dynasty. More than two thirds of Philae's surviving structures were built in the Ptolemaic era, during which the island became a prominent site of pilgrimage not only for Egyptians and Nubians but for pilgrims from as far as Anatolia, Crete, and the Greek mainland, gradually overtaking Elephantine as the most important sanctuary in southern Egypt. Among the inscriptions left by pilgrims are those left by three Romans at the first pylon in the summer of 116 BC, representing the oldest known Latin inscriptions in Egypt. The principal structures lay at the south end of the smaller island and included two colossal granite lions, a pair of obelisks each 13 metres high, pyramidal propyla of colossal dimensions, and small temples including one dedicated to Isis, Hathor, and deities related to midwifery covered with sculptures representing the birth of Ptolemy Philometor under the figure of Horus. The story of Osiris is everywhere represented on the walls of the temple, and the monuments attested beyond any others in the Nile valley the survival of pure Egyptian art centuries after the last of the Pharaohs had ceased to reign. Great pains were taken to mutilate the sculptures of the temple, attributable first to the zeal of early Christians and afterwards to the Iconoclasts, though images of Horus are often less mutilated than other carvings, presumably because early Christians saw parallels between the stories of Jesus and Horus. Several Roman emperors made artistic and architectural contributions to Philae, and during the Roman era the island was the site of the last known inscription in Egyptian hieroglyphs written in AD 394 and the last known Demotic inscription written in 452. Christianity was present at Philae by the fourth century, coexisting with traditional Egyptian religion, and according to the sixth-century historian Procopius the temple was closed officially in AD 537 by the local commander Narses in accordance with an order of Byzantine emperor Justinian I, an event conventionally considered to mark the end of ancient Egyptian religion. Five of its temples were subsequently converted into churches including the Temple of Isis dedicated to Saint Stephen, and two purpose-built churches were constructed on the north side of the island. In 1902 the Aswan Low Dam was completed, threatening to submerge the complex, and the island of Philae was nearly always flooded with the complex accessible only when the dam's sluices were open from July to October. In 1960 UNESCO started a project to save the buildings from the destructive effect of ever-increasing Nile waters, and following the decision to relocate rather than build protective dams around the island, a large coffer dam was constructed and the monuments were cleaned and measured using photogrammetry before every building was dismantled into about 40,000 units weighing between 2 and 25 tons and transported to the nearby island of Agilkia, situated on higher ground some 500 metres away. The contract for the execution of the works was awarded by UNESCO in 1974 to two Italian companies, Condotte Acque from Rome and Mazzi Estero from Verona, with the Italian architect Giovanni Joppolo entrusted with supervision, and the whole operation lasted from 1977 to 1980. The hieroglyphic reliefs of the temple complex are being studied and published by the Philae Temple Text Project of the Austrian Academy of Sciences in Vienna.

Temple_of_Kom_Ombo.location: Kom Ombo, Aswan Governorate, Upper Egypt
Temple_of_Kom_Ombo.detailed_knowledge:
The Temple of Kom Ombo is an unusual double temple in the town of Kom Ombo in Aswan Governorate, Upper Egypt. It was constructed during the Ptolemaic dynasty, 180–47 BC. Some additions to it were later made during the Roman period.

Architecture The building is unique because its 'double' design meant that there were courts, halls, sanctuaries and rooms duplicated for two sets of gods. The southern half of the temple was dedicated to the crocodile god Sobek, god of fertility and creator of the world with Hathor and Khonsu. Meanwhile, the northern part of the temple was dedicated to the falcon god Haroeris ("Horus the Elder"), along "with Tasenetnofret (the Good Sister, a special form of Hathor or Tefnut) and Panebtawy (Lord of the Two Lands)". The temple is atypical because everything is perfectly symmetrical along the main axis.

Decorations The texts and reliefs in the temple refer to cultic liturgies which were similar to those from that time period. The temple itself had a specific theology. The characters invoked the gods of Kom Ombo and their legend. Two themes were present in this temple: the universalist theme and the local theme. The two combine to form the theology of this temple. A temple was already built in the New Kingdom to honor these gods, however, this site gained in importance during the Ptolemaic Kingdom. Little remains of the New Kingdom temple. The existing temple was begun by Ptolemy VI Philometor (180–145 BC) at the beginning of his reign and added to by other Ptolemies, most notably Ptolemy XIII Theos Philopator (51–47 BC), who built the inner and outer hypostyles. The scene on the inner face of the rear wall of the temple is of particular interest, and "probably represents a set of surgical instruments".

Current state Much of the temple has been destroyed by the Nile, earthquakes, and later builders who used its stones for other projects. Some of the reliefs inside were defaced by Copts, who once used the temple as a church. All the temples buildings in the southern part of the plateau were cleared of debris and restored by Jacques de Morgan in 1893.

Crocodile Museum A few of the three hundred crocodile mummies discovered in the vicinity are displayed in The Crocodile Museum. In April 2018, the Egyptian Ministry of Antiquities announced the discovery of the head of the bust of Roman Emperor Marcus Aurelius during work to protect the site from groundwater. In September 2018, the Egyptian antiquities ministry announced that a sandstone sphinx statue had been discovered at the temple. The statue, measuring approximately 28 cm (11 in) in width and 38 cm (15 in) in height, likely dates to the Ptolemaic Dynasty.

Gallery See also List of ancient Egyptian sites, including sites of temples

References External links

fotopedia.com, Selected photos of the Temple of Kom Ombo

The Great Temple of Ramesses II.class_name: The Great Temple of Ramesses II
The Great Temple of Ramesses II.detailed_knowledge: Abu Simbel is a historic site comprising two massive rock-cut temples in the village of Abu Simbel, Aswan Governorate, Upper Egypt, near the border with Sudan, located on the western bank of Lake Nasser about 230 kilometres southwest of Aswan. The twin temples were originally carved out of the mountainside in the 13th century BC during the 19th Dynasty reign of Pharaoh Ramesses II, and their huge external rock relief figures of Ramesses II have become iconic. The complex was relocated in its entirety in 1968 to higher ground to avoid submersion by Lake Nasser, as part of the International Campaign to Save the Monuments of Nubia, under the supervision of Polish archaeologist Kazimierz Michalowski from the Polish Centre of Mediterranean Archaeology University of Warsaw. Abu Simbel and other relocated temples from Nubian sites such as Philae and Amada are part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site known as the Nubian Monuments. During his reign, Ramesses II embarked on an extensive building program throughout Egypt and Nubia, building several grand temples to impress upon the Nubians Egypt's might and to Egyptianize the people of Nubia, with the most prominent being the rock-cut temples near Abu Simbel at the Second Nile Cataract. There are two temples: the Great Temple dedicated to Ramesses II himself and to the gods Ra-Horakhty, Ptah, and Amun, and the Small Temple dedicated to his chief wife Queen Nefertari and the goddess Hathor. Construction of the temple complex started around 1264 BC and lasted for about 20 years until 1244 BC, and it was known as the Temple of Ramesses, Beloved by Amun. With the passage of time the temples fell into disuse and became mostly covered by sand, remaining unknown to Europeans until March 1813 when Swiss researcher Johann Ludwig Burckhardt found the small temple and top frieze of the main temple. Italian explorer Giovanni Belzoni entered the Great Temple on 1 August 1817 after 22 days of work, and in 1819 William John Bankes accompanied by Henry Salt undertook a thorough investigation to fully document the temples. In 1849 writer Maxime Du Camp photographed the temples as part of a mission commissioned by the French Ministry of Public Instruction, producing the first French printed book illustrated with photographs upon his return. The Great Temple, which took about twenty years to build and was completed around year 24 of Ramesses II's reign, is generally considered the grandest and most beautiful of the temples commissioned during his reign. Its single entrance is flanked by four colossal 20-metre statues each representing Ramesses II seated on a throne wearing the double crown of Upper and Lower Egypt, with smaller statues of his chief wife Nefertari, queen mother Mut-Tuy, his first two sons, and his first six daughters beside his legs. The facade behind the colossi is 33 metres high and 38 metres wide, carrying a frieze depicting twenty-two baboons worshipping the rising sun and a stele recording the marriage of Ramesses to a daughter of Hittite king Hattusili III. The hypostyle hall inside is 18 metres long and 16.7 metres wide, supported by eight huge Osirid pillars depicting the deified Ramesses linked to the god Osiris, with bas-reliefs on the walls depicting battle scenes including the famous Battle of Kadesh. The innermost sanctuary contains rock-cut sculptures of four seated figures: Ra-Horakhty, the deified Ramesses, Amun Ra, and Ptah. The axis of the temple was positioned so that on October 22 and February 22 the rays of the rising sun penetrate the sanctuary and illuminate the sculptures on the back wall, except for the statue of Ptah who always remains in the dark, dates allegedly corresponding to the king's birthday and coronation day. The Small Temple, built about 100 metres northeast of the Great Temple, was only the second time in ancient Egyptian history that a temple was dedicated to a queen, its rock-cut facade decorated with two groups of colossi of the king and his queen each slightly more than 10 metres high, remarkable as one of very few instances in Egyptian art where statues of the king and his consort are of equal size. In 1959 an international donations campaign to save the monuments of Nubia began as the construction of the Aswan High Dam threatened to raise the water level at Abu Simbel by up to 60 metres.
