After various proposals were considered, Egypt adopted a plan by Swedish engineering firm Vattenbyggnadsbyran to disassemble the temples into blocks and lift them to a higher location, estimated to cost 36 million US dollars. A joint venture of contractors led by German company Hochtief began work in 1963, constructing an 82-foot temporary cofferdam before removing approximately 300,000 tons of rock from the cliff above the temples and cutting them into 1,036 blocks, with the individual block weight not exceeding 20 tons for the temple chambers and 30 tons for the facades. The first block was lifted on 21 May 1965 and the last on 16 April 1966, with none having been seriously damaged. The temples were reassembled 208 metres further inland and 65 metres higher for the Great Temple and 67 metres higher for the Small Temple, with the orientation carefully duplicating the two occasions each year when the rising sun penetrates the innermost sanctum. Reinforced protective domes were constructed over the rear of each temple and an artificial hill built using 300,000 tons of material to recreate the original setting. The project was completed by early summer 1968, 18 months ahead of schedule, at a total cost of 41.7 million US dollars, of which half was borne by Egypt and half provided by international contributions from 48 countries. The success of the relocation helped the United Nations introduce the convention establishing the UNESCO World Heritage List. Today hundreds of tourists visit the temples daily, arriving by road from Aswan or by plane at Abu Simbel Airport, a domestic airfield constructed in 1969 specially to serve the temple complex.

Tutankhamun (King Tut).location: Valley of the Kings, West Bank of Luxor, Egypt
Tutankhamun (King Tut).detailed_knowledge: Tutankhamun or Tutankhamen, born around 1342 BC and dying around 1323 BC, was the antepenultimate pharaoh of the Eighteenth Dynasty of ancient Egypt, who ruled from approximately 1332 to 1323 BC. Born Tutankhaten, he instituted the restoration of the traditional polytheistic form of ancient Egyptian religion, undoing the previous shift to Atenism introduced by Akhenaten. His reign is considered one of the greatest restoration periods in ancient Egyptian history, and his endowments and restorations of cults were recorded on the Restoration Stela. The cult of the god Amun at Thebes was restored to prominence, and the royal couple changed their names to Tutankhamun and Ankhesenamun, replacing the Aten suffix. He moved the royal court from Akhenaten's capital at Amarna back to Memphis almost immediately upon his accession, reestablished diplomatic relations with the Mitanni, and carried out military campaigns in Nubia and the Near East. Tutankhamun was one of only a few kings known to have been worshipped as a deity during their lifetime, and he likely began construction of a royal tomb in the Valley of the Kings along with an accompanying mortuary temple, though both remained unfinished at the time of his death. He died unexpectedly at around eighteen years of age and, since his royal tomb was incomplete, was buried in a small non-royal tomb adapted for the purpose. He was succeeded by his vizier Ay, who had a short reign and was in turn succeeded by Horemheb, the former commander-in-chief of Tutankhamun's armed forces, under whom the restoration of traditional religion was completed and earlier Amarna Period rulers were erased from the record. Tutankhamun's tomb was discovered in 1922 by excavators led by Howard Carter and his patron George Herbert, the fifth Earl of Carnarvon; although it had clearly been raided in ancient times, it retained much of its original contents including the king's undisturbed mummy, and the discovery, yielding over 5,000 artifacts, gave rise to renewed worldwide public interest in ancient Egypt. His gold mask remains one of the most recognisable symbols of ancient Egyptian civilisation, and since the discovery of his tomb he has been referred to colloquially as King Tut. Tutankhamun was born during the reign of Akhenaten in the Amarna Period of the late Eighteenth Dynasty. His parentage is debated and not directly attested in surviving inscriptions, though he is referred to in a fragmentary inscription from Hermopolis as a king's son. He is generally thought to have been the son of Akhenaten or his co-ruler Smenkhkare, and DNA testing identified the mummified man from tomb KV55, thought to be either Akhenaten or Smenkhkare, as his father, while the anonymous mummy known as the Younger Lady from tomb KV35 was identified as his mother. Genetic research indicates his parents were full siblings, both being children of Amenhotep III and his chief wife Tiye, though the exact identity of the Younger Lady remains unknown and various scholars have proposed Nefertiti, Beketaten, Meritaten, or Meketaten as alternative candidates. When Tutankhaten became king he married Ankhesenpaaten, a daughter of Akhenaten who later changed her name to Ankhesenamun. He fathered two daughters who died at or soon after birth and were buried with him in his tomb, with CT studies revealing one was born prematurely at five to six months and the other at full term. Tutankhamun became pharaoh between eight and nine years of age following the short reigns of Smenkhkare and Neferneferuaten, taking the throne name Nebkheperure. In his third regnal year he reversed his father's religious policies, ending the worship of Aten, restoring Amun to supremacy, lifting the ban on the Amun cult and restoring its priesthood's privileges, and recording these actions on the Restoration Stela erected in his fourth regnal year, which was later usurped by Horemheb. He reburied his father's remains in the Valley of the Kings, and the royal court abandoned Amarna as Memphis became the main seat of administration. As part of the restoration of traditional cults he initiated building projects at Karnak, where he laid out the sphinx avenue leading to the temple of Mut, and at Luxor temple he completed the decoration of the entrance colonnade of Amenhotep III. He commissioned new statues of the deities and processional barques of cedar from Lebanon embellished with gold and silver, and enriched the priestly orders of the cults of Amun and Ptah. Tutankhamun was one of only a few pharaohs worshipped as a deity during his lifetime, evidenced by cult temples built in Kawa and Faras in Nubia dedicated to his deified form as a local incarnation of Amun, and by the Stela of Huy discovered at Karnak, which contains a hymn equating Tutankhamun with the god Amun. His deified cult had its own hierarchical structure, with Viceroy Huy serving as First Prophet of Nebkheperura, his brother Mermose as Second Prophet, and an official named Taemwadjsy managing a dedicated harem at Kawa. Regarding his health, two conditions have been confirmed through CT scans and genetic testing: a malarial infection and a compound left leg fracture with no signs of healing, suggesting the fracture occurred shortly before death. Various other diagnoses including gynecomastia, Marfan syndrome, clubfoot, and craniosynostosis have been proposed but subsequently rejected by genetic analysis, and the extensive walking sticks found in his tomb are now understood to have been royal regalia rather than mobility aids, as evidenced by their minimal wear patterns. He was of lean slender build and his mummy measured approximately 167 centimetres in height. Tutankhamun died unexpectedly and the most widely accepted conclusion is that his death resulted from the combination of his leg fracture and severe malarial infection. Earlier theories including murder by a blow to the head and death in a chariot accident have both been disproved by CT scan analysis. His funeral took place between late February and April based on analysis of funerary floral remains, with Ay performing the opening of the mouth ritual and being depicted presiding over the funeral, confirming his succession. Tutankhamun's mortuary cult was active after his death, attested by the stela of Userhat at the Metropolitan Museum of Art, and a mortuary temple at Western Thebes is well attested archaeologically, though it was likely destroyed by Horemheb and its remains have never been found. The succession following his death was complicated by the power vacuum he left, as both his known children died in infancy and he was presumably the last of the royal male line. His tomb KV62 in the Valley of the Kings was unusually small for a pharaoh of his status, likely because his unexpected death left no time to complete a grander tomb. It had been robbed at least twice in antiquity but retained the vast majority of its contents when Howard Carter discovered it on 4 November 1922 after a systematic search beginning in 1915. The tomb contained 5,398 items including a solid gold coffin, the gold face mask, thrones, archery bows, trumpets, a lotus chalice, furniture, food, wine, sandals, and linen, with Carter spending ten years cataloguing the contents. A dagger in the tomb was found to have a blade made from meteorite iron, and many burial goods showed signs of having been originally made for earlier owners such as Smenkhkare or Neferneferuaten. On 4 November 2007 Tutankhamun's mummy was placed on display in a climate-controlled glass case in his tomb, and in 2009 the tomb was closed for restoration before reopening in February 2019. International exhibitions of Tutankhamun's treasures began in Paris in 1962 and have since drawn tens of millions of visitors across Europe, North America, Asia, and Australia, with the Treasures of Tutankhamun tour of 1972 to 1979 attracting over 1.6 million visitors at the British Museum alone and more than eight million across the United States. The 2019 to 2022 King Tut: Treasures of the Golden Pharaoh tour featured one hundred and fifty gold artifacts before being interrupted by the COVID-19 pandemic. His treasures are now permanently housed in the Grand Egyptian Museum in Cairo.

Wadi Natrun Monasteries.location: Wadi El Natrun, Beheira Governorate, Egypt
Wadi Natrun Monasteries.detailed_knowledge:
Wadi El Natrun is a natural depression in northern Egypt situated west of the Nile Delta. The region lies below sea level and is distinguished by a series of alkaline lakes, extensive natron deposits, salt marshes, and areas of freshwater wetlands. Historically known as Scetis in Christian sources, the area became one of the most influential spiritual landscapes of the early Christian world. Today it remains renowned for its ancient monasteries, many of which continue to function as active religious communities. The surrounding desert is often referred to as the Desert of Scetis.

Geographically, Wadi El Natrun extends for approximately 55 to 60 kilometers and averages about 10 kilometers in width. It forms a closed depression within Egypt's Western Desert and contains numerous shallow lakes whose waters are highly concentrated with natron salts, giving them distinctive reddish-blue hues. Although commonly called a valley, the area is more accurately described as a depression because it lacks a permanent river system, tributaries, or outlet.

The region possesses a rich prehistoric heritage. Fossils dating to the Pliocene epoch have been discovered there, including remains of crocodiles, turtles, camels, ancient horses, hippopotamuses, antelopes, and relatives of elephants. These discoveries provide valuable evidence for the environmental conditions that existed in northern Egypt millions of years ago.

Wadi El Natrun was known to the ancient Egyptians and appears in early literary and religious sources. References to the area occur in Egyptian texts, including The Eloquent Peasant, and it was recognized among the important desert regions of ancient Egypt. Archaeological finds indicate that the area held religious significance from at least the second millennium BCE. Artifacts discovered in the region include royal inscriptions, architectural remains, and monuments associated with Egyptian rulers such as Amenemhat I. Historical evidence also suggests that the area witnessed conflicts between ancient Egyptians and Libyan groups, with military campaigns continuing into the reign of Ramesses III.

The economic importance of Wadi El Natrun stemmed largely from its abundant natron deposits. Natron played a vital role in the mummification process and was also used in the manufacture of Egyptian faience. During the Roman period, the region became an important center for glass production. Large industrial furnaces were constructed to manufacture raw glass that was exported throughout the Roman Empire. Archaeological excavations have revealed extensive evidence of this industry, demonstrating the region's role in long-distance trade networks. In the late nineteenth century, a narrow-gauge railway linked the area to surrounding regions and contributed to the development of tourism and commercial activity.

The greatest historical significance of Wadi El Natrun emerged during the rise of Christian monasticism. Beginning in the fourth century CE, the desert attracted large numbers of monks seeking solitude, spiritual discipline, and a life devoted to prayer. Saint Macarius of Egypt established one of the earliest monastic settlements in the region, and his example inspired many others to settle there. Over time, several major monastic communities developed, including those associated with Macarius, Bishoi, John the Dwarf, and Baramus. These communities evolved from scattered hermit cells into organized monasteries centered around churches and communal facilities.

Between the fourth and seventh centuries, the Nitrian Desert became one of the most important centers of Christian monastic life anywhere in the world. Thousands of monks gathered in the settlements of Nitria, Kellia, and Scetis, creating a vibrant spiritual culture that profoundly influenced Christian monastic traditions across the Mediterranean. Defensive walls and towers were eventually added to many monasteries in response to raids by desert tribes and periods of political instability.

Scetis suffered several devastating attacks during Late Antiquity, particularly from groups originating in the Libyan Desert. These invasions forced many prominent monks to abandon the region temporarily and marked a significant turning point in the history of early monasticism. Despite repeated destruction, the monastic communities were repeatedly rebuilt. While the neighboring centers of Nitria and Kellia were eventually abandoned, Scetis continued to survive throughout the medieval period.

Four major monasteries have remained active into the modern era: the Monastery of Saint Macarius the Great, the Paromeos Monastery, the Monastery of Saint Pishoy, and the Syrian Monastery. These institutions preserve centuries of religious tradition and continue to serve as important centers of Coptic spirituality. Many celebrated saints of the Desert Fathers movement lived and taught in the region, including Saint Macarius of Egypt, Saint Arsenius, Saint Moses the Black, Saint Pishoy, Saint John the Dwarf, and Saint Poimen.

Wadi El Natrun remains the foremost center of Coptic monasticism in Egypt and continues to attract pilgrims, scholars, and visitors interested in its religious heritage. Beyond its spiritual importance, the area is also associated with a notable episode in modern history. In 1935, French aviator and writer Antoine de Saint-Exupéry crashed in the desert near the region. After surviving the accident and enduring a life-threatening struggle against dehydration, he was rescued by a Bedouin. The experience later influenced his literary works and is often cited as one of the inspirations behind The Little Prince.

Today, Wadi El Natrun represents a unique combination of natural, archaeological, economic, and religious heritage. Its ancient lakes, prehistoric fossils, monastic settlements, and enduring cultural significance make it one of Egypt's most remarkable historical landscapes.

White Desert.detailed_knowledge:
Sahara el Beyda, the White Desert Protected Area, is a national park in Egypt, first established as a protected area in 2002. It is located in the Farafra depression, 45 km (28 mi) north of the town of Qasr Al Farafra. Part of the park is in the Farafra Oasis (New Valley Governorate). The park is the site of large white chalk rock formations, created through erosion by wind and sand. They are made of white calcium, quartz crystals, or limestone. The park is also the site of cliffs (at the northern end of the Farafra Depression), sand dunes (part of the Great Sand Sea), as well as Wadi Hennis and oases at Ain El Maqfi and Ain El Wadi. White Desert National Park covers an area of 300 km2 (120 mi2). The highest point in the park is at El Qess Abu Said at 353 m (1,158 ft) above sea level, and the lowest is at Wadi Hennis at 32 m (105 ft). The park serves as the refuge for various animals, including the endangered rhim gazelle and the vulnerable dorcas gazelle, as well as Barbary sheep; jackals; Rüppell's, red and fennec foxes; and the sand cat.

In popular culture In the Gathering Storm expansion pack released in 2019 for the turn-based strategy game Civilization VI, the Sahara el Beyda is featured as one of the Natural Wonders. Parts of the video for the song Suraj Hua Madhyam from the Bollywood film Kabhi Khushi Kabhie Gam were shot at Sahara el Beyda. The White Desert is featured as a playable region in the 2017 action-adventure video game Assassin's Creed: Origins.

Gallery References External links Ministry of Environment Egyptian Environmental Affairs Agency - Natural Protectorates Description Archived 2019-08-29 at the Wayback Machine

amenhotep iii and tiye.official_name: Colossal statue of Amenhotep III and Tiye
amenhotep iii and tiye.location: Egyptian Museum, Cairo (originally Medinet Habu, Western Thebes)
amenhotep iii and tiye.detailed_knowledge:
The colossal statue of Amenhotep III and Tiye is a monolith group statue of Egyptian pharaoh Amenhotep III of the eighteenth dynasty, his Great Royal Wife Tiye, and their daughter Princess Henuttaneb, mostly intact, along with two other daughters, partially destroyed. It is the largest known dyad ever carved. The statue originally stood in Medinet Habu, Western Thebes; today it is the centerpiece of the main hall of the Egyptian Museum in Cairo.

The statue is made of limestone, its width is 4.4 metres and its height is 7 metres. The almond-shaped eyes and curved eyebrows of the figures are of typical late 18th Dynasty style. Amenhotep III wears the nemes headdress with uraeus, a false beard, and a kilt, resting his hands on his knees. Queen Tiye is seated on his left, her right arm placed around her husband's waist. Her height is equal to that of the pharaoh, which reflects her prominent status. She wears an ankle-length, close-fitting dress and a heavy wig with a vulture headdress, modius, and double uraei. The cobras and the vulture are crowned: the right cobra wears the white crown of Upper Egypt, while the left one wears the red crown of Lower Egypt.

The three smaller figures depict three of the royal daughters. Princess Henuttaneb, standing between her parents, is depicted as a grown woman in a close-fitting dress and a full wig with modius and plumes, but without uraei — the only difference between her headdress and her mother's. Next to Amenhotep stands the damaged figure of a younger daughter, Nebetah, while next to Tiye stands the even more damaged figure of another princess whose name has been lost. The dyad is one of only two known statues depicting Henuttaneb, and the only known one of Nebetah.

The statue is likely to have been carved around the first Sed festival of Amenhotep III. Scholar Arielle Kozloff argues that the age of the daughters depicted, especially Henuttaneb, and the style of Queen Tiye's wig, which was at its most developed and nearly shrouded her face, suggest the statue was made during the third decade of the king's reign. It is possible it was carved from the high-quality limestone removed to create the open courtyard of TT192 — a large tomb belonging to Queen Tiye's steward Kheruef, work on which began around this time.

The eldest daughter of the royal couple, Sitamun, is absent from the statue group, probably because she had been elevated to the rank of Great Royal Wife by Year 30 of Amenhotep's reign. Henuttaneb was the second or third daughter. She is nowhere mentioned as a queen, but on this colossus she is described as "the companion of Horus, who is in his heart" — the only instance of this queenly title being given to a princess. Her name is sometimes written in a cartouche, which may indicate that she was elevated to queen status like Sitamun and Iset.

The statue belonged to the mortuary temple of Amenhotep III, which was, during its time, the largest temple complex in Thebes, surpassing even Karnak. Built too close to the floodplain, less than two hundred years later it already stood in ruins, and most of its stones were reused by later pharaohs for their own building projects. The place where the statue was found is likely to have been the south gate of the mortuary temple complex. Another possibility is that the dyad was usurped by Ramesses III when he used blocks from Amenhotep's temple to construct his own complex at Medinet Habu, though the original names were not erased.

The statue was found in the late 19th century in fragments. In 1897, the fragments were moved by Georges Daressy to the forecourt of the small Amun temple at Medinet Habu; later they were transported to Cairo and reassembled for the opening of the Egyptian Museum in 1902.

A 27.6 cm limestone head found in a private collection proved to be the head of Princess Nebetah. Nebetah wears a round wig and a modius headdress, and retains traces of the sidelock of youth. The head is believed to have been intentionally removed from the statue, probably during the medieval period. It was sold for US$206,500 in 2008, and an exact copy was subsequently attached to the statue in Cairo.

In 2011, several new fragments of the statue were discovered during a rescue excavation on the west bank of Luxor. These were reconstructed to form six missing parts, including portions of the king's headdress, chest, and right leg; the queen's wig, left arm, palm, fingers, and leg; and part of the statue base, which depicted a Nubian prisoner. With the newly found fragments added, the statue is now approximately 70 percent complete.

menkaure pyramid.detailed_knowledge:
The pyramid of Menkaure is the smallest of the three main pyramids of the Giza pyramid complex, located on the Giza Plateau in the southwestern outskirts of Cairo, Egypt. It is thought to have been built to serve as the tomb of the Fourth Dynasty King Menkaure.

Size and construction Menkaure's pyramid had an original height of 65.5 meters (215 ft), and was the smallest of the three major pyramids at the Giza Necropolis. It now stands at 61 m (200 ft) tall with a base of 108.5 m (356 ft). Its angle of incline is approximately 51°20 25 . It was constructed of limestone and Aswan granite. The upper portion was cased in the normal manner with Tura limestone. The construction of the outer casing of the pyramid was halted at sixteen to eighteen layers of granite following Menkaure's death, only seven of which remain today due to vandalism and erosion. Part of the granite was left in the rough. The descending passage to lower burial chamber is in the 'large apartment' floor, probably originally hidden by floor blocks. It is theorised that originally the pyramid was planned to be smaller, with the blind alley being the originally planned entrance passage. A boat hieroglyph was found painted on a limestone block near the pyramid, which may indicate the presence of a boat pit for a solar barque.

Age and location The pyramid's date of construction is unknown, because Menkaure's reign has not been accurately defined, but it was probably completed in the 26th century BC. It is a few hundred meters southwest of its larger neighbors, the pyramid of Khafre and the Great Pyramid of Khufu in the Giza necropolis. The pyramid was likely robbed as early as the First Intermediate Period, given that authors Bob Brier and Hoyt Hobbs claim that "all the pyramids were robbed" by the New Kingdom, when the construction of royal tombs in the Valley of the Kings began.

Sarcophagus and coffin In 1837, Howard Vyse and John Shae Perring discovered the basalt sarcophagus of Menkaure, described as beautiful black and rich in detail with a bold projecting cornice, which contained the bones of a young woman. It was loaded onto the ship Beatrice, but wrecked off the Spanish coast on the way to Great Britain.

A wooden anthropoid coffin inscribed with Menkaure's name and containing the remains of a mummy were found in the upper burial chamber and later transferred safely to the British Museum. The coffin was radiocarbon-dated to 1212–846 BC, from the late New Kingdom to the Third Intermediate Period. The male body and its shroud were carbon dated to the early Islamic Period.

Pyramid complex Recent archaeological findings from the Giza plateau show that pyramid construction relied on a Nile-connected harbor system to move large stones. Sediment cores and reconstructed waterways suggest that ports close to Giza operated through the Fourth Dynasty, allowing limestone blocks and materials to effectively reach the plateau. This harbor system supported not only the Khufu and Khafre's projects but also later construction at Menkaure's pyramid complex.

Pyramid temple In the mortuary temple, the foundations and the inner core were made of limestone. The floors were begun with granite and granite facings were added to some of the walls. The foundations of the valley temple were made of stone. Both temples were finished with crude bricks. Reisner estimated that some of the blocks of local stone in the walls of the mortuary temple weighed as much as 220 tons. The heaviest granite ashlars imported from Aswan weighed more than 30 tons. It is assumed that Menkaure's successor Shepseskaf completed the temple construction. An inscription was found in the mortuary temple that said he "made it (the temple) as his monument for his father, the king of upper and lower Egypt." Subsequent architectural additions and two stelae from the Sixth Dynasty suggest that a cult for the Pharaoh was maintained, or was periodically renewed, for two centuries after his death.

Valley temple The Menkaure Valley temple was excavated between 1908 and 1910 by American archaeologist George Andrew Reisner. He found a large number of statues, mostly of Menkaure alone, and as a member of a group. These were all carved in the naturalistic style of the Old Kingdom, with a high degree of detail.

Queens' pyramids South of the pyramid of Menkaure are three smaller pyramids, designated G3-a, G3-b, and G3-c, each accompanied by a temple and substructure. The easternmost is the largest and a true pyramid. Its casing is partly of granite, like the main pyramid, and is believed to have been completed due to the limestone pyramidion found close by. Neither of the other two progressed beyond the construction of the inner core. Reisner speculated that the structures were likely tombs for the queens of Menkaure, and that the individuals buried there may have been his half-sisters. The archaeologist Mark Lehner argues that pyramid G3-a has a layout akin to a ka pyramid, which would have housed a statue of the king rather than a body. The fact that the structure once contained a pink granite sarcophagus, has led scholars to speculate that it may have been reused as a queen's burial tomb, or that it served as a chapel where the body of Menkaure was mummified.

Attempted demolition In 1196, Al-Aziz Uthman, Saladin's son and the Sultan of Egypt, attempted to demolish the pyramids, starting with that of Menkaure. Workmen recruited to demolish the pyramid stayed at their job for eight months, but the task was expensive and slow. Workers were only able to remove one or two stones each day at most. Some used wedges and levers to move the stones, while others used ropes to pull them down. When a stone fell, it would bury itself in the sand, requiring extraordinary efforts to free it. Wedges were used to split the stones into several pieces, and a cart was used to carry it to the foot of the escarpment, where it was left. Despite their efforts, workmen were only able to damage the pyramid to the extent of leaving a large vertical gash at its northern face.

Restoration attempt In January 2024, a project to study and document the outer granite blocks which had fallen off of the facade began, with the goal to reinstall them. The project was planned to take three years to complete. Weeks after the announcement, the project was cancelled following backlash by researchers and a report by a team of experts called in by Egyptian officials and led by Zahi Hawass, Egypt s former minister of antiquities which unanimously objected to the re-installation of the granite casing blocks scattered around the base of the pyramid .

See also Egyptian pyramid construction techniques List of Egyptian pyramids List of megalithic sites

References Further reading Verner, Miroslav (2001). The Pyramids: The Mystery, Culture and Science of Egypt's Great Monuments. New York: Grove Press. ISBN 978-0-8021-1703-8. Lehner, Mark (1997). The Complete Pyramids: Solving the Ancient Mysteries. London: Thames & Hudson. ISBN 0-500-05084-8.

External links

Guardian's Ancient Egypt: The Pyramid of Menkaure NOVA Online – Pyramids: Menkaure's Inside Story

khufu statue.detailed_knowledge:
Khufu, known to the Greeks as Cheops, was the second pharaoh of Egypt's Fourth Dynasty during the Old Kingdom, ruling in the 26th century BCE. He succeeded his father Sneferu and is best known as the builder of the Great Pyramid of Giza, the largest and most famous pyramid in Egypt and the only surviving Wonder of the Ancient World.

Khufu's name means 'Khnum protects me,' reflecting his association with the creator god Khnum. Although he is one of the most famous rulers in history, surprisingly little contemporary information about his personality and reign survives. The only nearly complete surviving portrait of Khufu is a small ivory statuette discovered at Abydos in 1903.

Khufu belonged to a powerful royal family. He was traditionally considered the son of King Sneferu and Queen Hetepheres I. His family included numerous princes and princesses, among them Djedefre and Khafre, both of whom later became pharaohs. His reign marked the peak of Old Kingdom royal power and monumental construction.

The most significant achievement of Khufu's reign was the construction of the Great Pyramid at Giza. Originally standing about 147 meters high, it was the tallest human-made structure in the world for nearly four thousand years. The pyramid complex included temples, subsidiary pyramids for queens, cemeteries for royal relatives and officials, boat pits, and extensive administrative facilities.

Recent archaeological discoveries, especially the Diary of Merer found at Wadi al-Jarf on the Red Sea coast, provide valuable evidence about the logistics of pyramid construction. These papyri describe the transportation of limestone blocks from Tura to Giza and are among the oldest known papyrus documents ever discovered.

Khufu maintained trade and mining expeditions throughout Egypt and neighboring regions. In Sinai, his expeditions extracted turquoise and copper, while trade with Byblos supplied valuable cedar wood from Lebanon for shipbuilding and royal projects.

Although later Greek historians such as Herodotus and Diodorus portrayed Khufu as a harsh and oppressive ruler, archaeological evidence from ancient Egypt suggests a far more complex picture. Numerous mortuary cults dedicated to him continued for centuries after his death, indicating that he remained respected within Egyptian religious tradition.

Khufu's legacy endured throughout Egyptian history. During the Middle Kingdom and New Kingdom he was remembered in literature, religious traditions, and historical records. Stories about him appear in the Westcar Papyrus, where he is depicted as curious, intelligent, and interested in wisdom and prophecy.

Today Khufu remains one of the most famous figures of ancient Egypt. His Great Pyramid continues to be a symbol of Egyptian civilization, engineering achievement, and royal authority. The discoveries associated with his reign have provided crucial insights into the administration, economy, religion, and construction techniques of the Old Kingdom.
